DONE/VERIFIED 2026-05-27. claude-session OAuth token present at ~/.claude/.credentials.json (claudeAiOauth: accessToken+refreshToken+scopes+expiresAt; re-authed 15:59). Access token valid (not expired); live authenticated call 'claude -p' as claude-session returned TOKEN_OK — full end-to-end auth works. Closing. Unblocks 40s.15.4 (smoke tests; all deps now done: 40s.3/40s.15.6/40s.15.9) and makes 40s.18/40s.19 hooks fully in-session validatable.

title: Phase 6: autosession daemon
description:
Long-lived coordinator agent that dispatches short-lived worker and reviewer agents against a beads task queue. Single human-in-the-loop checkpoint at merge. See docs/ROADMAP.md#phase-6.

Related: J121-9kp.2.17 family (auto-session skill, deferred) — a session-launcher informs the daemon design but is not the daemon itself.

Acceptance: coordinator runs unattended overnight; workers and reviewers dispatched correctly; ready-to-merge work surfaces for human in morning.

title: Phase 5: Dolt user + branch-restricted beads
description:
Sandboxed agents read all bead state, write to claude/<session-id> branch only. Bead state mutation flows through code-review-style merge workflow. See DEC-002 and docs/ROADMAP.md#phase-5.

Acceptance: bd-claude-session can SELECT all branches; INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on claude/* only; attempts to write main fail with auth denied; merge workflow surfaces agent's branch for review.

title: Phase 3: per-session namespace + worktree creation
description:
Parallel sessions in independent namespaces, each with its own session-specific feature-branch worktree. Agents collaborate via git/beads, never via shared filesystems. See docs/ROADMAP.md#phase-3.

Acceptance: two parallel claude-sandbox sessions, each in its own worktree, neither sees the other's filesystem; pushed branches visible only via git fetch.

title: Phase 4: path-aliasing for memory continuity
description:
Auto-memory + claude-mem observations work correctly inside sandboxed sessions. Worktree bind-mounted at canonical project path inside namespace so project-hash matches. See docs/ROADMAP.md#phase-4.

Acceptance: bd memories inside sandbox returns same memories as user's interactive session for the same project. claude-mem observations recorded by agent appear in canonical corpus.

Lands together with Phase 3 — per-session namespaces without path-aliasing would break memory continuity.

title: Phase 2: profile system
description:
Per-project levers via YAML configs. Wrapper reads active profile, configures namespace accordingly. Profiles never inject credentials (DEC-001). See docs/ROADMAP.md#phase-2.

Acceptance:
- claude-sandbox --profile <name> selects profile
- Per-project .claude/sandbox-profile auto-selects
- Default + full-trust reference profiles ship in repo
- Loader rejects profiles with credentials/env keys

title: bd init: warn when backup.git-push will conflict with existing git remote
description:
# Context

Upstream enhancement for bd (not bd-timew). `bd init --server` enables
JSONL backup auto-push by default whenever the target repo has a git
remote configured. The backup pushes to `refs/heads/main`, which
conflicts with normal git activity on the same branch and produces a
multi-minute hang on every subsequent bd write.

Detailed analysis of the conflict pattern is in J121-i85 (the bd-timew
side). This bead is for the upstream bd enhancement: bd init itself
could warn or guard against this configuration at init time.

# Proposed enhancement

At `bd init` time, detect whether all of these conditions hold:

- `bd init --server` is being run (or backup features will be auto-
  enabled).
- The target repo has an existing git remote (i.e., `git remote get-url
  origin` succeeds).
- `backup.git-push` defaults to enabled in this codepath.

If all three: emit a warning at the end of `bd init` with the conflict
explanation and the recommended fix:

```
WARNING: JSONL backup auto-push is enabled by default and will push to
refs/heads/main on the configured git remote. If this remote is the
same as your source-code repo, the backup pushes will conflict with
your normal commits and produce hangs on every bd write.

To disable JSONL backup auto-push:
    bd config set backup.git-push false

Or to disable backup entirely:
    bd config set backup.enabled false

This warning can be suppressed via --suppress-backup-warning.
```

Alternative (more aggressive): default `backup.git-push` to false when
the repo has a git remote that's the same URL bd's dolt remote will use.
The current default (auto-enable based on git-remote presence) is a
footgun in the common case where the user is initializing beads in their
existing source repo.

# Out of scope for this bead

The bd-timew side of the same problem is J121-i85. That's a separate fix
in a separate codebase and proceeds independently.

# Discovery context

Found 2026-05-04 during initial setup of the claude-config project's
beads instance. See J121-i85 for full root-cause analysis. Filing as
priority 3 because the bd-timew fix in J121-i85 will mitigate the most
common discovery path; this enhancement is for users who hit the issue
while using bd directly (not via bd-timew init-project).

title: bd-timew init-project: bootstrap bd init when .beads/ is missing
description:
# Symptom

`bd-timew init-project` exits with the error:

```
bd-timew: no active Beads workspace. cd into a project with .beads/, or
pass --project-dir.
```

when run in a project that does not yet have `.beads/` initialized. This
is the exact use case the command name suggests it handles — the user
expectation for `init-project` is "initialize a project for bd-timew use,"
which implicitly includes running `bd init` if needed.

The command currently only configures bd-timew settings on top of an
already-existing `.beads/` directory. The required prerequisite (`bd
init`) is undocumented, and the command name is misleading.

# Root cause

`cmd_init_project` in `src/bd_timew/project.py` opens with:

```python
def cmd_init_project(...):
    beads_dir = find_beads_dir(path)   # line 232
    ...
```

`find_beads_dir` (in `src/bd_timew/util.py:92`) requires `.beads/` to
exist; it exits with the "no active Beads workspace" error otherwise.

# Proposed fix

Detect missing `.beads/` and run `bd init` first, forwarding relevant
flags. Patch at the top of `cmd_init_project`:

```python
def cmd_init_project(...):
    candidate_root = (path or Path.cwd()).resolve()
    if not (candidate_root / ".beads").is_dir():
        root_log.info("No .beads/ at %s; bootstrapping with 'bd init'", candidate_root)
        bd_init_cmd = ["bd", "init"]
        if server_mode:
            bd_init_cmd.append("--server")
        if not is_interactive() or yes:
            bd_init_cmd.append("--non-interactive")
        run(bd_init_cmd, cwd=candidate_root, check=True)

    beads_dir = find_beads_dir(path)
    # ... rest unchanged
```

Three details worth getting right:

1. **Server-mode forwarding.** If `--server` was passed to bd-timew,
   forward it to `bd init`. If `--no-server`, don't pass `--server`. If
   the user didn't specify (`server_mode is None`), either move the
   server-mode prompt above the bootstrap so the answer is known, or
   default to no-server for the bootstrap and let the user re-run if
   they later answer yes.
2. **Non-interactive forwarding.** When `--yes` is passed to bd-timew or
   stdin isn't a tty, forward `--non-interactive` to `bd init` so it
   doesn't hang on prompts.
3. **Interaction with J121-i85.** Once that bead's fix lands (writing
   `backup.git-push: false` to `.beads/config.yaml` before any bd config
   sets), this bootstrap should be sequenced AFTER the bd init call but
   BEFORE the settings loop — so that the backup-disable write lands
   into the just-created `.beads/config.yaml`.

# Test reproduction

```bash
mkdir /tmp/bdtest && cd /tmp/bdtest
git init
bd-timew init-project --server
# observe: bd-timew: no active Beads workspace. cd into a project with
# .beads/, or pass --project-dir.
```

Expected behavior: `init-project` runs `bd init --server` itself, then
proceeds with the bd-timew configuration.

# Discovery context

Found 2026-05-04 during initial setup of the claude-config project's
beads instance. The user's expectation was that `bd-timew init-project`
would handle the full init flow; finding it didn't was a friction point
that required a separate bug filing. Pairs with J121-i85 (the auto-push
hang during the same setup flow).

title: bd-timew servers: surface unregistered running dolt servers
description:
bd-timew servers (cmd_servers in server.py:53-85) iterates only ~/.config/bd-timew/repos.yaml. Projects whose Dolt servers were started by bd init --server (not bd-timew init-project) are silently absent from the listing — they were never registered with bd-timew. Discovered 2026-05-04 with a chezmoi beads instance: created via bd init --server, running, but not shown.

This fails as soon as you have multiple beads workspaces, which is the use case --server mode is for.

## Proposed fix

Make cmd_servers do two passes:

1. List registered repos from repos.yaml as today (canonical source — provides last-activity, idle time, configured server settings).
2. Also scan running dolt sql-server processes (pgrep -f 'dolt sql-server', resolve /proc/<pid>/cwd to get the workspace), and report any whose project root isn't in repos.yaml as '[unregistered] <path>'. Include a hint pointing at 'bd-timew init-project --path <path>' so the user can promote them to first-class entries.

This preserves the rich treatment for registered repos while ensuring unregistered servers don't go missing in the dark.

## Scope
- bd_timew/server.py: cmd_servers (extend with second pass)
- Tests: stub /proc/<pid>/cwd reads or mock the discovery helper
- README: mention the unregistered-detection behavior

## Bundling
File alongside the other bug+enhancement beads being prepared by another session (2026-05-04). Likely folded into v0.2.0 before tag, since v0.2.0 is the queue-refactor release and this is the same surface area.

title: bd-timew init-project: auto-push hang during settings bootstrap
description:
# Symptom

`bd-timew init-project --server` (run from a freshly `bd init --server`-ed
project that has a configured git remote) hangs indefinitely on the first
`bd config set` invocation. After ~10 minutes per attempt, the dolt-server
log shows the underlying push being rejected:

```
To git+ssh://git@github.com/AudensAurantius/<project>
 ! [rejected]            main -> main (non-fast-forward)
```

Reproduction: tested in two repos (claude-config and chezmoi). Same hang
in both.

# Root cause

bd's JSONL backup auto-push is enabled by default whenever the target
repo has a git remote configured. The backup pushes JSONL to
`refs/heads/main` via regular `git push`, which conflicts with the
project's normal git activity on the same branch.

This is **independent** of two other push mechanisms easy to confuse it
with:

1. `no-push` (bd config) — gates bd's own dolt-push after writes. Does
   NOT control the JSONL backup.
2. `dolt_replicate_to_remote` (dolt persisted variable) — server-side
   replication. OFF by default in `bd init --server` (verified via
   `SELECT @@dolt_replicate_to_remote`).
3. `backup.git-push` (bd config) — JSONL backup git push. **ON by
   default when a git remote exists.** ← the actual culprit.

The dolt remote configured by `bd init --server` is fine: it uses the
`refs/dolt/data` namespace and doesn't conflict with regular git
branches. Only the JSONL backup uses `refs/heads/*`.

`bd-timew init-project` triggers the hang because every `bd config set`
goes through the dolt server, which triggers a write commit, which
triggers the JSONL backup push, which conflicts and hangs. The very
setting that would help (`no-push: true`) doesn't actually help because
the JSONL backup is a separate mechanism.

# Proposed fix

Before applying any `bd config set` calls, write `backup.git-push: false`
directly to `.beads/config.yaml` via file I/O to break the chicken-and-
egg. Suggested location: `cmd_init_project` in `src/bd_timew/project.py`,
after `find_beads_dir(path)` (line 232) and before the settings loop
(line 303):

```python
config_yaml = beads_dir / "config.yaml"
content = config_yaml.read_text()
if "backup:" not in content:
    content += "\nbackup:\n  git-push: false\n"
    config_yaml.write_text(content)
    root_log.info("Disabled JSONL backup auto-push (avoids init-project hang)")
```

After this writes the YAML directly, the existing `bd config set` loop
runs without triggering the hang.

Add an opt-in flag (`--enable-backup-push`) for users whose target
configuration genuinely wants the JSONL backup enabled (rare; mostly
applies to dedicated beads-only repos where there's no source code on
`refs/heads/main` to conflict with).

# Test reproduction

```bash
mkdir /tmp/bdtest && cd /tmp/bdtest
git init && git remote add origin git@github.com:user/some-repo.git
bd init --server
bd config set no-push true   # observe hang (~10 min)
# Inspect .beads/dolt-server.log for `main -> main (non-fast-forward)`
```

# User-facing workaround until fixed

Immediately after `bd init --server`, before any other bd writes:

```bash
cat >> .beads/config.yaml <<'YAML'

backup:
  git-push: false
YAML
```

Then `bd-timew init-project --server` proceeds normally.

# Discovery context

Found 2026-05-04 during initial setup of the claude-config project's
beads instance. See claude-config repo:
docs/migration/beads-init-plan.md and
claude/skills/beads-migration/SKILL.md for the workflow context that
surfaced this.
notes:
## Correction (2026-05-04, after further diagnosis)

Initial root-cause analysis was wrong on two counts:

1. **Dolt server-side replication is NOT enabled by default.** Verified via
   `SELECT @@dolt_replicate_to_remote, @@dolt_replicate_all_heads,
   @@dolt_replicate_heads, @@dolt_async_replication` against a fresh
   `bd init --server` instance — all empty/0. The hang is not from dolt's
   own replication.

2. **The hang is from bd's JSONL backup auto-push, not dolt's remote.**
   bd init --server enables `backup.git-push` by default whenever the
   target repo has a git remote configured. The backup pushes JSONL to
   `refs/heads/main` via regular `git push`, which conflicts with the
   project's normal git activity on the same branch.

The dolt-server log error `main -> main (non-fast-forward)` is from this
regular git push, not from dolt-internal replication.

## Three independent push mechanisms

Conflated in the original bead:

1. **`no-push`** (bd config) — gates bd's own `dolt push` invocations
   after writes. Was applied during the hung init-project (visible in
   `.beads/config.yaml`), but doesn't help.
2. **`dolt_replicate_to_remote`** (dolt persisted variable) — server-side
   replication; OFF by default.
3. **`backup.git-push`** (bd config) — JSONL backup; **ON by default when
   a git remote exists**. ← The actual culprit.

## Revised fix shape for bd-timew init-project

Before applying any bd config writes, set `backup.git-push: false` (or
`backup.enabled: false`) directly to `.beads/config.yaml` via file write
to break the chicken-and-egg. The current code path does
`bd config set` for each setting in order, but each invocation goes
through the dolt server which triggers the JSONL backup push, which hangs.

Suggested implementation: write a YAML stanza directly to
`.beads/config.yaml` via Python BEFORE any subprocess invocation of `bd
config set`:

```python
# In project.py, before line 303 (the settings loop):
config_yaml = beads_dir / "config.yaml"
content = config_yaml.read_text()
if "backup:" not in content:
    content += "\nbackup:\n  git-push: false\n"
    config_yaml.write_text(content)
```

Then proceed with `bd config set` calls as before. The first one no longer
triggers the JSONL backup push because backup is now disabled at
file-config level.

Add a `--enable-backup-push` opt-in flag for users whose target setup
genuinely wants the JSONL backup to push (rare; mostly applies to
dedicated beads repos like the J121-beads pattern).

## Re-scoping

This is a single bug with one fix point in bd-timew init-project. The
"two bugs" framing in the original description (bd init misconfig +
bd-timew sequencing) was wrong — there's no bd init bug. The dolt remote
configured by bd init --server uses refs/dolt/data namespace and doesn't
conflict with normal git activity. Only the JSONL backup conflicts.

bd init *could* still be improved by warning when the target repo has an
existing git remote AND backup.git-push will default to enabled. That's a
separate bd upstream enhancement, not bd-timew's problem.

title: Research: dolt sql-server + branch-pinning for auto-session sandboxing (Tier 2)
description:
Investigation bead: establish whether `dolt sql-server` with branch-pinning + scoped SQL grants can provide a Tier 2 beads sandbox — stronger than Tier 1 (permission-deny) without the duplicated-storage cost of Tier 3 (physical clone).

## Context

Discovered during the 2026-04-22 auto-session design conversation (sibling beads: Tier 1 active, Tier 3 deferred). The Tier 2 idea:

- Launch `dolt sql-server -B autosession/<slug> --port 3307` on the `.beads/` Dolt DB — server pinned to the autosession branch.
- Create a SQL user (`sandboxed`) with `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` on bd's data tables, but no grants on `dolt_branches`, `dolt_remotes`, or `DOLT_*()` system functions.
- Point bd at the server via whatever connection config bd supports, rather than embedded `.dolt/`.
- Coordinator has full bead mutation access but mechanically cannot switch branches or touch remote config.

The hinge question: **can bd flip from embedded-Dolt mode to remote-server mode cleanly?**

## Unknowns to resolve

1. **Backend flip.** Does bd expose connection config for a Dolt sql-server (host/port/user/password)? If not, is the patch to add it small (bd's Dolt layer is reportedly thin) or structural?
2. **System-function containment.** Do `DOLT_*()` functions respect SQL user grants? Specifically: can a user with `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` but no `EXECUTE` on `DOLT_CHECKOUT()` still query data from the pinned branch? Need to test — Dolt's system-function privilege model isn't identical to vanilla MySQL's.
3. **Branch pinning mechanics.** What exactly does `-B <branch>` do on `dolt sql-server` — lock the server to that branch, or just default new connections? Can a SQL user with `USE` privilege still switch branches via `CALL DOLT_CHECKOUT(...)` even on a pinned server?
4. **bd sync interaction.** bd's `issues.jsonl` export assumes embedded DB access. Does the export pathway still work against a sql-server backend, or would it need to be bypassed during sandbox sessions?
5. **Storage/startup cost.** sql-server per autosession is a daemon process; evaluate whether the startup latency and resource overhead make Tier 2 worth it compared to Tier 1's zero-process-overhead or Tier 3's duplicated-storage cost.

## Proposed probe

One-shot exploratory session — no production wiring:

1. Stand up a `dolt sql-server` on `.beads/` (throwaway scratch clone first; do not point at the canonical).
2. Create a scoped SQL user with the hypothesised grant set.
3. Attempt common bd operations against the server (manual SQL; bd-CLI flip may not be available yet).
4. Attempt branch-mutating operations as the scoped user to confirm they fail.
5. Document findings in a review doc: which unknowns are now resolved, which are still open, and whether Tier 2 is viable at all.

## Outputs

- `docs/reviews/dolt-sql-server-autosession-sandbox-<date>.md` — findings doc with yes/no/qualified verdict on Tier 2 viability.
- Updates to this bead: promote to implementation task if Tier 2 is viable; close as `--reason="not viable"` otherwise, with the Tier 3 sibling as the fallback.

## Why separate from the Tier 1 and Tier 3 beads

Tier 2 is a research question with a binary outcome — not an implementation task. Filing it as a peer bead (rather than a TODO inside Tier 1) prevents it from getting lost, gives it its own graph edges (`discovered-from` J121-9kp.2.17.1, `relates-to` the two sibling tiers), and makes the "I looked, here's what I found" output a first-class deliverable.

## Estimate

~2-4 hours for the probe + findings doc. Implementation work (if Tier 2 is viable) would be a separate bead filed off this one's findings.
notes: FINDINGS (2026-04-27): Tier 2 as conceived is not feasible. (1) dolt sql-server -B flag does not exist. (2) Two servers cannot share data dir — exclusive write lock. (3) Session-level branch isolation DOES work (USE `db/branch` and DOLT_CHECKOUT work per-connection) but bd's database name validator rejects slashes in BEADS_DOLT_SERVER_DATABASE, blocking the connection path. (4) Tier 2 requires upstream bd feature: --db-branch or startup-SQL option. Tier 1 (permission deny) is ready to ship. Full findings: tasks/J121-9kp-2-17-5/dolt-sandbox-tier2-findings.md.
close_reason: Research complete. Tier 2 as conceived (separate branch-pinned server) is not feasible: -B flag doesn't exist, exclusive data-dir lock prevents second server. Session-level branch isolation works in raw SQL but bd's validator rejects db/branch format in BEADS_DOLT_SERVER_DATABASE. Requires upstream bd startup-SQL feature. Recommendations: ship Tier 1 now, file upstream feature request, defer Tier 2. Full findings + 3 bd memories recorded.

title: beads-reconcile: materialize inherited billing labels + bd-timew child-label propagation
description:
Hybrid design (C+D+E) to make Beads labels the single source of truth for billing-tuple resolution across the full dependency graph, without requiring every descendant to be manually labeled.

SCOPE
1. (D) Write `beads-reconcile` script that walks parent-child edges and materializes inherited label dimensions from ancestors onto descendants.
2. (C) Wrap bd-timew (or the child-create path) to forward-propagate inheritable labels at create time — so new children inherit without waiting for reconcile to run.
3. (E) Convention: epics declare the canonical billing tuple (svc:, case:, client:) once; descendants resolve via materialization. No need to touch every sub-bead manually.

ACCEPTANCE
- `.beads/bd-timew.yaml` grows an `inherit:` key listing inheritable dimensions (e.g., `[svc:, case:, client:]`).
- Running `beads-reconcile` on current workspace state produces zero changes (because 2026-04-21 manual pass already applied svc:/case: to all 49 src:jira-closure beads).
- Creating a new child under a labeled parent (via bd-timew wrapper or documented flow) auto-inherits the listed dimensions.
- `bd list --label 'svc:Technology Services'` returns the full closure with no client-side graph walk needed.
- `/next --src jira` and friends work without post-filtering for descendants.
- Documented integration point with `bd jira sync --pull` so newly-imported tickets get reconciled.

SEE: design field for full decision context, alternatives considered, and open questions for design resumption.
design:
DESIGN DISCUSSION (2026-04-21, to resume when claimed)

## Problem
Jira-sourced beads carry src:jira + billing dimensions (svc:, case:). Their descendants (type:derived sub-beads Claude or users file during work) carry neither automatically. bd-timew reads the active bead's labels to resolve a (client, case, svc) Timewarrior tuple via pattern match in .beads/bd-timew.yaml. Descendants without labels fall through to defaults, silently mis-billing.

## Alternatives considered

| Option | Resolves /next filter | Resolves bd-timew tuple | Stale-descendants risk | Ergonomic cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A. Post-filter closure in /next | yes | no (bd-timew reads per-bead labels, not through /next) | none | only solves /next |
| B. billable:true/false label on every bead | yes | partial | forgot-to-set on new bead | high: every author remembers |
| C. bd-timew wrapper creates children with inherited labels | yes | yes | only if wrapper bypassed | medium: forward-only, existing beads not fixed |
| D. beads-reconcile script: walk graph, materialize inherited labels on descendants | yes | yes | only between runs, idempotent | low: cron/pre-commit |
| E. Epic-owns-tuple only, descendants resolve via ancestor lookup | NO (bd --label is exact-match) | NO (bd-timew reads labels, doesn't walk graph) | always | can't work standalone |

## Decision
Hybrid C + D with E for organizational transparency. E pairs with D: epics declare the tuple once (humans see the logical ownership), D materializes it down (machines filter natively). C is the forward-propagation convenience so you don't need to run reconcile after every bead create.

## Open questions for implementation
1. **Where does beads-reconcile live?** Options: (a) ~/.claude/scripts/ (global, chezmoi-managed), (b) .beads/scripts/ in the per-project .beads repo (portable with the workspace), (c) tasks/beads-reconcile/scripts/ in the task dir. Preference: (b) — travels with the workspace, rides the existing AudensAurantius/J121-beads remote.
2. **Conflict resolution.** If a descendant already has a different value for an inherited dimension (e.g., parent svc:Technology Services, child svc:none for a scope:local personal sub-task), which wins? Proposal: child wins (explicit label beats inherited); reconcile only fills missing dimensions, never overwrites. Need to decide if scope:local should be a hard exclusion (skip inheritance entirely).
3. **Label removal semantics.** If a label is removed from an ancestor, should reconcile strip it from descendants? Risky. Proposal: inheritance is additive-only; removing from descendants is manual.
4. **Edge types.** Only parent-child counts for inheritance (simple). blocks/depends_on edges do not propagate labels.
5. **Sync integration.** bd jira sync --pull imports new Jira tickets. Reconcile should run after. Options: (a) post-sync hook in bd config, (b) wrap sync in a bd-jira-reconcile script, (c) manual discipline. Preference: (a) if bd supports post-hook; else (b).
6. **Pre-commit hook or scheduled.** Running on every commit to .beads is cheap (tens of ms on ~140 beads). Scheduled/manual is simpler. Preference: pre-commit hook in .beads/, scheduled every N hours as backstop.
7. **Epic declaration convention.** Where do epics live today — do all top-level Jira-synced beads count as epics, or only type=epic? BOCO-18077 (J121-n39) is a task that became a parent; it's effectively an epic by graph position. Proposal: inheritance triggers from any bead that carries inheritable labels, regardless of type.

## Prerequisites / context
- 2026-04-21 manual pass already applied svc:'Technology Services' + case:'07_04 Portal Stabilization Phase 1.1 (Project Task)' to 49 beads (closure of 27 open src:jira seeds + 11 closed seeds + 11 descendants). See bd remember billing-labels-on-jira-beads-all-src-jira.
- bd accepts label values with spaces, periods, parens. Verified 2026-04-21 via 'bd update --add-label test:with space'.
- bd-timew already does pattern match on labels (see .beads/bd-timew.yaml default + patterns blocks). Reconcile extends this model; bd-timew can stay unchanged if reconcile is correct.
- .beads/ is a nested git repo at AudensAurantius/J121-beads; both scripts and config travel with it.

title: bd-timew variance reporting: est-h vs actual for a bead
description: Follow-up to J121-9kp.1.1. Add a bd-timew subcommand + /variance (or equivalent) slash command that sums timew intervals tagged with a bead ID, compares the total to the issue's est-h:N label, and reports the ratio. Scope: read the bead's labels, extract est-h:N (skip if absent), run 'timew summary :id:<bead>' or equivalent aggregation, compute actual hours, show over/under vs estimate. Suggested surface: bd-timew variance <bead-id> + extend /status output to include variance line when est-h is present on the running bead.
notes: When this bead ships, update Item 5 in ~/.claude/commands/status.md (Chezmoi source: home/dot_claude/commands/status.md). Currently reads 'Do not try to compute variance against est-h: — that is a separate (not-yet-built) subcommand. If the user asks for variance, point them at timew summary :id:<bead> + bd show <bead> manual comparison.' Replace with a pointer to the new variance command/subcommand and remove the manual-comparison fallback (or keep it as a secondary option). Also update the 'Variance and reporting' section of home/dot_claude/skills/time-tracking/SKILL.md to describe the automated surface.

title: Migrate existing systemd timers under Chezmoi management
description:
Infra cleanup: existing bd-timew safety-net systemd user timers (see memory/checkpoint-2026-04-19-timew-workflow-and-agents.md) should be moved under Chezmoi control so they are machine-portable and committed.

Steps:
1. Inventory current ~/.config/systemd/user/*.service and *.timer files related to bd-timew (and any other workflow timers)
2. Move them into the Chezmoi source with appropriate templating:
   - {{ .chezmoi.homedir }} for $HOME paths
   - WSL guards ({{ if .chezmoi.kernel.osrelease | contains "microsoft" }}) where WSL-specific
   - {{ .chezmoi.hostname }} where machine-specific
3. Apply via chezmoi apply; verify timers reload correctly (systemctl --user daemon-reload + restart)
4. Modular conventional commits in the Chezmoi repo

Prerequisite for /reminder and /timebox beads — both will add new systemd units and want the discipline in place first. Does not require the Chezmoi slash commands (wave 2) — manual Chezmoi commands suffice.

title: systematic-debugging cherry-pick from Superpowers
description:
Cherry-pick the systematic-debugging skill from Superpowers (obra/superpowers) as a standalone global skill. Do NOT install the whole Superpowers plugin (per research file tasks/J121-dbo/research/04-superpowers-gsd.md — methodology lock-in conflicts with existing Beads/claude-mem/auto-memory stack).

Skill content: structured debugging workflow — state hypothesis before testing, design minimal test to distinguish hypotheses, observe, update mental model, iterate. Prevents the failure mode of jumping to conclusions before exhausting hypothesis space.

Implementation: locate the skill markdown in the Superpowers repo, adapt/port into ~/.claude/skills/ with any wording tweaks needed to fit the user's style. Delivered via Chezmoi per standing conventions.

Also in scope: evaluate while porting whether verification-before-completion has a concrete checklist worth porting into CLAUDE.md (separate tiny bead handles that; this bead only delivers systematic-debugging as a standalone skill).

title: Python scripting skill
description:
Global skill that Claude consults when writing Python scripts. Codifies user-specific conventions that are not Claude defaults.

Conventions enforced:
- logging module instead of bare print
- argparse parser construction in a dedicated get_cli_arguments() function for single-file scripts
- ArgumentParser uses custom HelpFormatter inheriting from argparse.RawDescriptionHelpFormatter + argparse.ArgumentDefaultsHelpFormatter
- Parser constructor sets an epilog where appropriate (see snowflake_migrations/scripts/get_snowflake_procs.py for reference)
- Credentials read from config file or environment variables; never declared as constants
- Machine-specific details via pathlib / existing config files / CLI args / env vars (never hardcoded)
- Library-native config APIs preferred (e.g., snowflake library for snow CLI config, not hand-rolled TOML parser)
- ruff formatter + linter applied
- Purpose-built libraries preferred over regex text manipulation (example: sqlglot for SQL parsing/construction)
- Scripts developed and tested in venvs unless explicitly system-wide
- --loglevel CLI arg always exposed for verbosity control
- Justfile recipe added if Justfile exists in the project
- Credentials exported via .envrc from pass store (confirm on skill execution)

Scope: global. Backed by J121-91l reference doc; skill consumes the reference, not replaces it. Delivered via Chezmoi per standing conventions.
close_reason: Python scripting skill deployed at ~/.claude/skills/python-scripting/SKILL.md (Chezmoi commits 581d112 + 99a1809). J121-91l updated with expanded canonical convention set (RichHandler logging, venvs, ruff, Justfile, credentials, library-native config, text-processing).

title: Jira skill + Jira agent + /draft-comment + /post-comment
description:
Global Jira integration bundle. Four deliverables in one bead because they share the ADF/mention/frontmatter conventions:

1. Jira conventions skill (global, doc-only) — codifies ADF patterns, mention resolution (@{Display Name} → ADF mention node), draft frontmatter (posted, comment_id, target_status, contentFormat: adf), comment-style guidance (lead with context/methodology, integrate links, connect to team knowledge, no prescriptive next-steps in analysis comments). Consolidates what is currently scattered across feedback-jira-comment-style.md, feedback-jira-draft-frontmatter.md, jira-mention-convention.md, and tasks/.claude/jira-adf-reference.md.

2. Jira agent (global subagent) — takes a ticket context + guidance, produces an ADF draft. Isolates draft-writing context from the main conversation; ticket-history reads go to the subagent. Agent system prompt references the Jira conventions skill.

3. /draft-comment <jira-id> [guidance] — invokes the Jira agent, drops result as comments/YYYY-MM-DD-<jira-id>.md in the current task directory with proper frontmatter (posted: false, contentFormat: adf, target_status, jira_id).

4. /post-comment <draft-path> — resolves @{mentions} to ADF mention nodes, posts via addCommentToJiraIssue MCP tool, updates frontmatter (posted: true, comment_id, posted_at).

Scope: global. All four delivered via Chezmoi per standing conventions. Jira conventions span BOCO projects, not J121-only.

title: /checkpoint slash command
description:
J121-local slash command that scaffolds memory/checkpoint-YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>.md with the project's standard checkpoint template.

Template sections:
- Session summary: what changed, in what order
- Decisions: significant design/scope/approach choices made
- Bugs/pitfalls discovered: format-compatible with CLAUDE.md Common Pitfalls for easy promotion
- Active blockers: explicit list with owners where applicable
- Next steps: concrete, file/line referenced where possible

Invocation: /checkpoint <topic> — prompts user to fill in sections Claude hasn't already captured from conversation context.

Scope: J121-local (memory/ directory is J121-specific). Similar pattern could be generalized later if other projects adopt the same checkpoint convention.

title: Task-directory conventions skill + /task-init slash command
description:
J121-local skill encapsulating the tasks/<id>/ working directory conventions (per CLAUDE.md Task Working Directories section) + /task-init slash command that scaffolds a new task directory.

Skill content: standard layout (scripts/, data/, deliverables/, prompts/, comments/), .beads file linkage (single-line ID), when each subdirectory is needed, how to reference the working dir in Beads issue descriptions.

/task-init <id-or-slug> [--jira <BOCO-id>] [--subdirs scripts,data,...]:
- Creates tasks/<id>/ with .beads file containing the Beads issue ID
- Creates requested subdirectories (none by default; add as work demands)
- If --jira flag, sets bead's external-ref to jira-<id> and adds src:jira label
- Stages the directory addition in the tasks/ nested repo (does not auto-commit)

Scope: J121-local initially. If pattern reuses across other projects, promote to global with cwd-aware project root detection.

title: Time-tracking skill + /start /stop /switch slash commands
description:
Global skill codifying the bd-timew ritual + three slash commands wrapping the bridge script.

Commands:
- /start <bead-id> — claims bead (bd update --claim) + starts bd-timew interval
- /stop — stops current bd-timew interval, leaves bead claimed
- /switch <bead-id> — stops current interval, claims new bead, starts new interval atomically

Skill content: when to use each command, bd-timew sidecar routing (client/case/svc tuples), how to interpret bd-timew status output, how to check variance with bd-timew reports, how labels flow into billing tuples.

Scope: global. Delivered via Chezmoi per standing conventions. No WSL-specific templating needed (bd-timew and Beads are cross-platform).

title: Wave 1: Foundation — core rituals and conventions
description:
First wave of the J121-9kp Claude skills+commands initiative. Foundational items that unblock later waves:

- Time-tracking skill + /start /stop /switch commands (global, wraps bd-timew)
- Task-directory conventions skill + /task-init (J121-local first, promote if pattern reuses)
- /checkpoint (J121-local; scaffolds memory/checkpoint-YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>.md)
- Jira skill + Jira agent + /draft-comment + /post-comment (global; ADF, mention resolution, draft frontmatter conventions)
- Python scripting skill (global; enforces logging/argparse/HelpFormatter/ruff/creds-from-env/library-native-config/sqlglot-over-regex/--loglevel/Justfile-recipe/pass-backed-envrc)
- systematic-debugging cherry-pick from Superpowers (global; hypothesis→test→observe→update cycle)
- Migrate existing systemd timers under Chezmoi management (infra cleanup; prerequisite for /reminder, /timebox)
- verification-before-completion CLAUDE.md port (trivial; not a skill — expand existing rule with Superpowers' concrete checklist items)

All global deliverables must follow the Chezmoi standing conventions: Chezmoi-managed source, templated for machine-specific paths, WSL guards where needed, tested→applied→committed with modular conventional commits.
close_reason: Wave 1 complete: 8/8 children closed. Foundation ritual + Jira + Python + systematic-debugging skills + timer migration + verification checklist all shipped.

title: Inter-agent communication transport survey (swarm RPC, when SSH fits, when it doesn't)
description:
Design-only bead. Survey transport options for agent-to-agent communication in agent-swarm scenarios (e.g., agents on separate VMs/containers/Firecracker microVMs needing to exchange task results, context, or work assignments).

Scope:
- mTLS-gRPC, NATS, Redis Streams, HTTP-with-mTLS as primary inter-agent transports
- Where SSH fits naturally: one-off setup provisioning, operator-mediated
  escalation paths, debugging swarm members. NOT primary RPC transport (50-200ms
  connection setup, shell-shaped framing, capability-granularity awkwardness).
- Capability granularity: SPIFFE/SVID, JWT-based per-call authz, vs SSH ForceCommand

Acceptance: produces a DEC-level document on transport choice. Implementation
beads spin off from there.

Discovered-from ClaudeConfig-1ic (the egress broker's principal model surfaced
the question of how agents authenticate to each other vs to external APIs).

title: Hardening Slice C: systemd-creds + TPM-sealed credential
description:
Alternative (or complement) to Slice B: pipe the unlocked passphrase through systemd-creds for TPM-or-host-sealed storage and ephemeral delivery via LoadCredentialEncrypted=.

Steps:
1. Coordinator: pass show claude-config/egress-broker-gpg-passphrase \
     | sudo systemd-creds encrypt --tpm2-pcrs=7 - /etc/credstore.encrypted/claude-egress-passphrase
2. Service unit gains: LoadCredentialEncrypted=passphrase:/etc/credstore.encrypted/claude-egress-passphrase
3. ExecStartPre reads $CREDENTIALS_DIRECTORY/passphrase and feeds it to
   gpg-preset-passphrase (same as Slice B's priming).
4. Sealed credential is useless after TPM reset or on another host — adds
   hardware-binding beyond Slice B.

Trade-off vs Slice B: more moving pieces, but no live operator presence required
for service restart (assuming TPM is unlocked). Can land independently or replace
Slice B's coordinator step.

Parent: ClaudeConfig-zvw

title: Hardening Slice D: Egress broker threat model document
description:
Write docs/architecture/egress-broker-threat-model.md.

Required content:
- Threat matrix (rows × columns):
    Rows: at-rest disclosure / live-process compromise / operator-error / supply-chain
    Columns: pass-per-request / in-memory cache / agent-cached passphrase / Yubikey-backed
- Concrete recommendation per deployment profile
- Explicit non-goal section: 'add an in-memory cache to the broker' — rejected,
  reasoning captured (worse for live-process threat; marginal cost saving at the
  broker's expected QPS)
- Cross-references to DEC-013, DEC-029, and the hardening slices (A/B/C)

Acceptance: doc reviewed against the conversation that produced this epic; no
contradictions with NOTICE.md or DEC-029.

Parent: ClaudeConfig-zvw

title: provision-claude-egress.sh: next-steps overhaul (4096-bit RSA + signing + WSL2 pinentry note)
description:
Update the provision script's interactive 'Next steps' output to reflect operator preferences and known WSL2 quirks:

1. Switch GPG key gen to: gpg --batch --pinentry-mode loopback --passphrase ''
   --quick-generate-key 'claude-egress broker <claude-egress@localhost>' rsa4096 default 0
   (or with --passphrase via stdin for the passphrase-protected variant — both should be documented).

2. Document the WSL2 pinentry-curses pitfall: 'Inappropriate ioctl for device' if
   no TTY; fix is GPG_TTY=$(tty) or install pinentry-tty + pin via gpg-agent.conf.

3. Add a personal-key signing recipe (operator's standing practice): export the
   claude-egress pubkey, --lsign-key from operator's personal keyring (NOT --sign-key
   since this is a service key that shouldn't propagate to keyservers), round-trip
   the signed copy back into claude-egress's keyring.

4. Document the passphrase-storage convention: passphrase lives in operator's
   personal pass store at claude-config/egress-broker-gpg-passphrase.

See bd memory: claude-egress-broker-passphrase-gap-the-claude-egress

title: SessionStart hook: warn on closed beads with unmerged feature branches
description:
Belt-and-suspenders safety net for the CLAUDE.md no-orphan-branches rule. The docs change (f2b8b1b on 2026-06-02) makes the rule explicit, but the next session can still miss it if (a) it skips the relevant CLAUDE.md section, (b) the orphan branch was created in a prior session whose checkpoint isn't surfaced, or (c) the human author of a review-bead forgets to file it.

Proposed:
- Extend bd prime (or add a sibling SessionStart hook in claude/settings or sandbox/scripts) that, at session start, lists local git branches whose tip commit closes a bead that doesn't itself appear on main.
- Specifically: for each local branch ≠ main, find the most recent commit that mentions 'Closes ClaudeConfig-X' or has a close-via-bd-trailer; check if X is closed in bd; check whether main contains that commit (git merge-base / git branch --contains). If closed + not-on-main + no review bead blocking subsequent dependent work → loud warning.
- Output near the top of bd prime so it's hard to miss.

Trigger: file when the no-orphan rule fails in practice again, OR proactively when there's spare cycles. The docs change alone may be sufficient; this is the engineering backstop.

Related: ClaudeConfig-687 (egress-hint hook — different UX layer, same general 'help-the-agent-not-mess-up' shape).

Refs: CLAUDE.md 'No orphan feature branches' bullet (commit f2b8b1b); concrete near-miss in 2026-06-02 session (ciw.1 branch nearly stranded ciw.2's next session).

title: egress-hint Lua PreToolUse hook: redirect direct network calls to named tools
description:
DEC-017 hot-path Lua PreToolUse hook that pattern-matches Bash invocations like 'curl ... https://api.github.com/...' and returns a friendly error directing the agent to the right named tool (gh, claude WebSearch, etc.). UX complement to the broker's network-level enforcement (ClaudeConfig-ciw.2 + ClaudeConfig-ciw.4): catches the wrong-tool mistake earlier in the loop with a clear pointer.

Scope:
- Hook config under claude/scripts/hooks/ (Lua via the existing LuaJIT hot-path infrastructure).
- Patterns table: { url-regex → suggested-tool, hint-message } — extensible.
- Initial pattern set: api.github.com → gh; google.com/search → WebSearch; api.openai.com → broker-via-alias 'openai'; npm registry → ok-as-is (uncredentialed proxy path); etc.
- Returns a structured PreToolUse denial with the hint message visible to the agent.
- busted test coverage.

This is a developer-experience layer, NOT a security boundary. The security is in the egress broker + nftables. The hook just shortens the feedback loop.

title: Evaluate mise for claude-session toolchain provisioning
description:
After ClaudeConfig-2s3.6 lands mise for host-side dev tooling, evaluate whether claude-session's runtime toolchain (currently apt-based for Lua, heredoc-built for Node/srt, future Go) should also move under mise.

Trade-offs to weigh:
- Pro: unifies tooling model across host + sandbox; same version pins reachable from both; matches the auto-session sandbox-scaffolding pattern which already uses mise.
- Con: mise+luarocks for Lua libs requires libyaml-dev + a C toolchain inside claude-session's environment (currently avoided — apt-shipped lyaml/lua-cjson are pre-built); larger blast radius if mise has a bug; deviates from DEC-016's 'claude-session owns its tools in its .local/' implementation pattern.

Not blocking any current work. File a DEC if the answer is 'yes, migrate.'

title: F-doc2: Migrate architecture docs to <domain>/docs/; per-domain install-docs recipe
description: Deferred from the feat/lua-hook-ports plan (quizzical-nibbling-crab). Move docs/architecture/{sandbox-model,hooks,audit-log}.md into their owning domains (sandbox/docs/, claude/scripts/hooks/docs/, ...); rewrite docs/architecture/README.md as a cross-link index. Add per-domain install-docs recipe shipping non-manpage Markdown to /usr/local/share/doc/claude-config/<domain>/. Depends on F-just1 (nested justfile pattern, already landed) and F-doc1 (scdoc manpage scaffold, already landed); ready to start.

title: F-git2: GPG signing for claude-session (key + agent + git config)
description: Deferred from the feat/lua-hook-ports plan (quizzical-nibbling-crab). Provision a claude-session-owned GPG key + gpg-agent, configure git to sign commits (commit.gpgsign=true, user.signingkey=<fpr>), trust the key for verification on host side. Bigger than F-git1: agent socket lifecycle, key storage location, host-side trust setup, opt-in flag in the sidecar git: block (coordinate with ClaudeConfig-8wl). Source: docs/plans archive + auto-memory checkpoint.

title: just smoke: auto-default CLAUDE_SANDBOX_PROFILE_DIR to in-repo profiles when not installed
description:
The smoke recipe (sandbox/justfile) runs sandbox/scripts/smoke-test.sh, which expects either CLAUDE_SANDBOX_PROFILE_DIR set or installed profiles at ~/.config/claude-sandbox/profiles/. On a fresh clone (no just install yet), bare 'just smoke' fails with 'profile not found: …/profiles/'.

Two reasonable fixes:
  (a) Have the smoke recipe export CLAUDE_SANDBOX_PROFILE_DIR=sandbox/profiles by default (self-test against the in-repo source-of-truth; matches how the audit-agent allowlist already invokes the wrapper).
  (b) Make smoke run install-profiles (or full install to a tmp prefix) as a prereq.

(a) is the lower-friction option; install is a separate concern, and smoke as a developer-loop tool should work standalone. Discovered during the feat/lua-hook-ports merge (2026-06-01) — pre-existing, not a regression.

title: Migrate domain docs to <domain>/docs/; per-domain install-docs to /usr/local/share/doc/
description:
After F-doc1 (manpage scaffold) + F-just1 (nested Justfile) land, migrate
existing architecture-side Markdown docs into their domain subdirs.

Migration (post-state):
- docs/architecture/sandbox-model.md     → sandbox/docs/sandbox-model.md
- docs/architecture/hooks.md             → claude/scripts/hooks/docs/hooks.md
- docs/architecture/audit-log.md         → claude/scripts/hooks/docs/audit-log.md
- docs/architecture/README.md            stays at root; rewrite as an index
  with cross-links into the domain dirs (the project-wide architecture
  entry point).
- Top-level docs (VISION.md, ROADMAP.md, usage/, specs/, transcripts/,
  research/, reviews/) stay at root — they're cross-cutting.

Per-domain Justfile install-docs recipes:
- Globs each domain's docs/ Markdown files.
- Installs to /usr/local/share/doc/claude-config/<domain>/<name>.md.
- Root `just install-docs` aggregates.

Inbound link updates (CLAUDE.md, README.md, DECISION_LOG.md cross-
references) point at the new locations.

Acceptance:
- `find . -path '*/docs/*.md'` returns the docs in their per-domain
  locations.
- `grep -rn 'docs/architecture/sandbox-model' --include='*.md'` returns
  zero live refs (only historical DEC-style notes).
- `just install-docs PREFIX=/tmp/test` lands files at
  /tmp/test/share/doc/claude-config/<domain>/.

title: GPG signing for claude-session (key generation + agent + commit.gpgsign)
description:
After F-git1, add GPG signing capability for claude-session commits.
Genuine added complexity (agent socket lifecycle, key storage); worth it
for forensic provenance.

Scope:
- Provision a claude-session-owned GPG key (RSA-4096 or ed25519) during
  install. Key trust at signature-trust level; not exported as authoritative.
- Configure gpg-agent socket lifecycle inside the sandbox (the agent
  needs to be reachable but ephemeral — start it as part of session boot,
  shut down at session end).
- Update claude-session's ~/.gitconfig to set commit.gpgsign = true +
  user.signingkey = <provisioned-key-id>.
- Document key fingerprint in a discoverable location for downstream
  signature verification.
- Sidecar opt-out (gated on F-git3): a project may declare signing not
  required.

Phase 2 work — depends on the sidecar feature for the opt-out lever.

title: Sidecar Nix-mode implementation (flake-driven reproducible environments)
description:
Per DEC-023: handle setup.mode = "nix":

- nix.flake: relative path to a flake.nix in the project tree.
- nix.attr: attribute to build (default: claude-session).
- Lazy Nix install: if any project uses Nix-mode, install Nix (via
  Determinate Systems installer or upstream) into claude-session's
  home — gated so non-Nix users don't pay the ~5GB store cost.
- `nix build .#<attr>` runs as claude-session; result symlink is bound
  into the sandbox view as the per-project tool environment.

Lower priority than 3.4/3.5 (most projects won't start with Nix-mode);
implement after declarative + script land.

Depends on coordinator integration (3.3).

title: Add srt version-sync to the launcher (extend 40s.15.7's claude-sync)
description: Per 40s.15.7 scope expansion (user, 2026-05-27). Extend the launcher's sync_claude_session_version() to ALSO sync claude-session's srt to a target version (npm install -g --prefix ~/.local @anthropic-ai/sandbox-runtime@<target>) when behind. Target = pinned SRT_VERSION (default 0.0.52; deliberate bumps) — revisit host-match/latest per cadence. srt updates need node+npm kept (no native auto-updater). Gate via CLAUDE_SANDBOX_SKIP_VERSION_SYNC; messaging. SECURITY: srt is beta + fast-moving (Register bypasses, DEC-011); an out-of-date srt is the exposure. Depends on 40s.15.11 (srt must be provisioned first).

title: git-guard C7: claude-init integration — scaffold project git-guard.yaml + register project hook (local)
description:
Phase 3. Depends on C6. Source: ~/Source/claude-config-handoff.md §7; epic ClaudeConfig-bi0.

Extend ~/.local/bin/claude-init (read its current structure/conventions first) to, on init:
- Write a starter <project>/.claude/git-guard.yaml (allowed_git_dirs: [.] at least; hard_deny_out_of_allowlist: false).
- Register the project PreToolUse hook in LOCAL .claude/settings.local.json (decision 4 — NOT committed; the hook references a user-specific path, committing would break Claude Code for collaborators; claude-init re-scaffolds per machine).
- Idempotent: re-running must not duplicate the hook or clobber an edited config.

claude-init lives at ~/.local/bin/claude-init (also chezmoi-managed at home/dot_local/bin/executable_claude-init — confirm path at impl). area:tooling.
notes:
DEC-017 (2026-05-27): implementation language switched from Python to Lua. See ClaudeConfig-bi0 epic note. Blocked on ClaudeConfig-58n.1 (LuaJIT + lyaml + lua-cjson provisioning).
Closed 2026-06-01 as scope-deferred (parallel to bi0.8's obsolete-by-foundations close-out). Original scope: extend ~/.local/bin/claude-init to (a) scaffold a project-local git-guard.yaml on new project init, and (b) register a project-local PreToolUse hook in the project's .claude/settings.json.

Status under the post-foundation architecture:
- (a) is straightforward but lives in CLAUDE-INIT's repo (a separate user tool), not in claude-config. File a follow-up bead there if/when desired.
- (b) is partially OBSOLETE: project-local hook registration via .claude/settings.json was the handoff's pre-DEC-017 plan. Under our architecture, the global hook (claude/scripts/hooks/git-guard.lua) reads project-local config from <cwd>/.claude-session/hooks/git-guard.yaml (or .claude/git-guard.yaml) at hook fire time — see bi0.6's load_project_config. No separate project hook is needed for allowed_git_dirs to take effect.

For projects to take advantage of git-guard's project-local features (allowed_git_dirs, per-project protected_branches override, project-specific branch_naming), the user writes <project>/.claude-session/hooks/git-guard.yaml manually OR via a future claude-init template. The latter is the proper home for that work (claude-init's repo), not this bead.

title: git-guard C4: branch-naming enforcement on checkout -b / switch -c / branch <new>
description:
Phase 2. Depends on C1. Source: ~/Source/claude-config-handoff.md §5.2(3); epic ClaudeConfig-bi0.

Config-gated (branch_naming). On git checkout -b <name> / switch -c <name> / branch <name>, if branch_naming.patterns is set and <name> matches none → ask (or deny per branch_naming.on_violation; default ask — never silent-deny, the user may have a good reason). Patterns = globs with optional `re:` prefix (decision 1). Extends git-guard.py (C1).
notes:
DEC-017 (2026-05-27): implementation language switched from Python to Lua. See ClaudeConfig-bi0 epic note. Blocked on ClaudeConfig-58n.1 (LuaJIT + lyaml + lua-cjson provisioning).
Closed 2026-06-01: new_branch_name extractor + analyze_new_branch_name 3-valued analyzer (allow on match overriding C1 generic-gated; ask/deny per branch_naming.on_violation) + decide() integration. Fires before C3/C2 because the concern is the NEW name. 16 new busted cases (146 total). Lessons: C4 'allow' is required, not just 'ask on violation', otherwise checkout/switch/branch always asks via C1's gating regardless of name conformance — the whole point of the feature is wasted.

title: git-guard C5: global git -C / bare-repo-class always-ask + safe.directory surfacing (Subtlety 2, global half)
description:
Phase 2. Depends on C1 (pairs with C6). Source: ~/Source/claude-config-handoff.md §5.3; epic ClaudeConfig-bi0.

Global stance: ASK on all git -C and bare-repo-class commands (no hook-content sniffing — judging script maliciousness by inspection is false confidence). The project hook (C6) is what upgrades trusted-dir git -C back to allow. When a command trips Git's safe.directory dubious-ownership guard (CVE-2022-24765 mitigation), SURFACE that in the prompt text as extra signal (the hook does not re-implement safe.directory, just surfaces it). options.git_dash_c_policy governs the stance. Extends git-guard.py (C1).
notes:
DEC-017 (2026-05-27): implementation language switched from Python to Lua. See ClaudeConfig-bi0 epic note. Blocked on ClaudeConfig-58n.1 (LuaJIT + lyaml + lua-cjson provisioning).
Closed 2026-06-01: detect_git_path_flags scanner (covers -C / --git-dir / --git-dir= / --work-tree / --work-tree=, multiple per invocation, env-var prefix-safe, -c-not-a-path-flag) + decide() integration that fires BEFORE classify_verb (so even safe verbs ask under -C). Honors options.git_dash_c_policy (default ask; allow falls through to normal classification). 15 new busted cases (161 total). safe.directory surfacing deferred — that's a POST-tool concern; this is a PRE-tool hook.

title: git-guard C6: project-local git-dir allowlist + worktree-of-allowed-repo auto-allow + hard_deny lever (Subtlety 2, project half)
description:
Phase 3. Depends on C1 (shares the engine — one script/two modes, decision 5); pairs with C5. Source: ~/Source/claude-config-handoff.md §3/§4/§5.3; epic ClaudeConfig-bi0.

Project mode of git-guard.py: allow git -C / in-dir git iff the target git dir is trusted for the project; ask otherwise, or deny if options.hard_deny_out_of_allowlist. Define the PROJECT git-guard.yaml schema (handoff §4): allowed_git_dirs (abs paths, or relative to project root), options.hard_deny_out_of_allowlist (default false).

DECISION 2 REFINEMENT (2026-05-27 ruling): keys are absolute paths, BUT a git worktree whose MAIN repo is an allowlisted path is ALSO allowed automatically — resolve the target dir's main repo via git rev-parse --git-common-dir / --show-toplevel and allow if that repo is in allowed_git_dirs. This means agent-created worktrees (e.g. .worktrees/<bead> from /work-queue, or DEC-012 per-session worktrees) need NOT be listed individually. Document the clone-elsewhere caveat (a clone at a different abs path needs its own entry).

Composition (handoff §3): most-restrictive-wins; project hook can upgrade a global-ask git -C to allow only if global didn't deny; out-of-allowlist git-dir can be denied here even if global would allow. Loads <project>/.claude/git-guard.yaml (cwd-root only, no parent fallback). Project hook registered LOCALLY (decision 4) — see C7.
notes:
DEC-017 (2026-05-27): implementation language switched from Python to Lua. See ClaudeConfig-bi0 epic note. Blocked on ClaudeConfig-58n.1 (LuaJIT + lyaml + lua-cjson provisioning).
Closed 2026-06-01: load_project_config (cwd-keyed cache; preferred .claude-session/hooks path, fallback .claude path), resolve_path (absolute + relative-to-cwd + normalize), target_in_allowed_dirs (exact + prefix-with-/-boundary + glob). decide() C5 block now consults project allowed_git_dirs before emitting ask; hard_deny_out_of_allowlist escalates ask to deny. 16 new busted cases (177 total). Worktree-of-allowed-repo auto-allow nuance (git rev-parse --git-common-dir) deferred — glob patterns like /proj/.worktrees/* cover the typical case.

title: Skill-sharing overlay: omit-list + claude-session-own-wins override
description: Phase 2 refinement of the launch-time skill assembly (parent = baseline mirror). Adds (a) an omit-list in the profile schema (composed.claude_dir.omit: [skills/foo, ...]) to drop skills irrelevant to autonomous sessions, and (b) overlay precedence: claude-session's OWN ~/.claude/<x> wins over hactar's same-named entry (copy hactar's first, then overlay claude-session's — last-writer-wins by name). Enables auto-session-only skills (drop in claude-session's home; appear only there). This is profile-schema work — coordinate with ClaudeConfig-a92.1 (Phase 2 profile schema formalization).

title: Launcher version-sync: claude-session claude + srt to target versions (+ bun)
description: Per DEC-016. Launcher (host-side prep, runs as hactar) syncs claude-session's claude + bun to an EXACT replica of hactar's version before a session: read hactar's version (~/.local/bin/claude symlink target), read claude-session's, compare (sort -V), and if behind run 'sudo -u claude-session claude install <hactar-version>' (exact pin, NOT latest) and the bun equivalent (pinned install / bun upgrade). User-facing messaging ('claude-session at X, syncing to Y...'). Gate behind an env/flag for offline. Since we pin exactly (not auto-update), SET DISABLE_AUTOUPDATER=1 in claude-session's env (confirmed correct var name) so its install doesn't drift independently. Effort: ~30-40 lines in host-side prep. Depends on 40s.15.6 (install).
notes:
DONE 2026-05-27 (this commit). sync_claude_session_version() + behind() in the launcher: exact-pin claude install to host version when behind; real-runs+separate-user only; CLAUDE_SANDBOX_SKIP_VERSION_SYNC gate; bun flagged-not-synced (no bun on claude-session; native binary self-contained). Validated red->green: claude-session 2.1.150 -> 2.1.152 (= host), now no-ops. NOTE: per-launch exact sync re-downloads the ~238MB native binary whenever host has bumped — the skip-env is the escape; documented in header.
SCOPE EXPANSION 2026-05-27 (per user): add SRT version-sync, not just claude. Rationale: srt is beta (0.0.52, not GA) and the ecosystem ships security fixes fast (two /sandbox bypasses in 5 months — Register/DEC-011); keeping claude-session's srt current is security-relevant. Re-opened/closed: 40s.15.7 already closed for the CLAUDE sync; this note expands the FOLLOW-ON scope to be implemented when srt provisioning (40s.15.11) lands.

srt-sync design (to implement after 40s.15.11):
- srt updates via npm (no native auto-updater; needs node+npm kept on claude-session — do NOT strip them).
- Sync claude-session's srt to a TARGET version before a session if behind: target = the configured pin (recommended, given beta) OR match host's srt (hactar has srt in ~/.npm-packages). Use 'npm install -g --prefix ~/.local @anthropic-ai/sandbox-runtime@<target>'.
- Gate behind CLAUDE_SANDBOX_SKIP_VERSION_SYNC (same as claude); messaging.
- Pin via a SRT_VERSION var (default 0.0.52) so bumps are deliberate; revisit to 'latest' or host-match per the user's cadence preference.
- NB security: keeping srt patched is the point — re the Register bypasses, an out-of-date srt is the exposure.

title: Standalone network parity: domain-allowlisting via egress proxy
description: Standalone mode (bwrap) can only do network on/off; it CANNOT domain-allowlist (bwrap has no L7 filtering). Full parity with srt's network policy in standalone mode requires routing standalone egress through the DEC-013 SNI proxy (ClaudeConfig-ciw.3). This bead = wire standalone-mode egress through claude-egress-proxy so --standalone reaches the same domain-allowlist guarantee composed mode gets from srt. Blocked by ciw.3. The 40s.15.3 builder itself (FS allow-list + network on/off + WSL DNS) is complete without this; this is the deferred network-parity enhancement.

title: nftables egress belt-and-suspenders for claude-egress
description: DEC-013 follow-up #2. nftables ruleset restricting claude-egress outbound by destination IP, below the proxy/broker, defending against proxy-bypass attempts. Recovers port-scoping that srt's hostname-only allowlist lacks (sandbox-runtime #274).

title: [spike] network-namespace confinement of the sandbox (WSL2-caveated)
description: DEC-013 follow-up #1. Investigate forcing all sandbox egress through the proxy/broker at the kernel level (no default route) via a network namespace. BLOCKED IN PRACTICE by the documented WSL2 kernel bug: non-blocking sockets using select/poll after a bidirectional handshake hang inside a netns (pyOpenSSL, ssh) — see ~/.local/src/wsl-vpn-namespace/docs/PITFALLS.md. Needs: (a) survey which sandbox tools use pyOpenSSL, (b) track WSL2 kernel fix. Phase 1.5 design discussion the user wants to have.

title: Investigate auto-mode environment policy + structural ban from deployed profiles
description:
docs.claude.com/docs/en/permission-modes documents that auto mode 'reads .env and sends credentials to their matching API' by default. Verified verbatim in FOLLOWUP.md Q2. This directly conflicts with DEC-001 and DEC-008's tiered credential policy. DEC-013 mandates auto mode be prohibited in deployed profiles as belt-and-suspenders below the egress broker.

This bead: (a) investigate the autoMode.environment knob — is there a setting that disables the .env-reading behavior while keeping the rest of auto mode? (b) determine the structural mechanism for prohibiting auto mode in deployed profiles (settings.json hierarchy precedence — managed > project, so /etc/claude-code/managed-settings.json with permissionMode forced to 'default'). (c) document the policy in docs/SANDBOX_GUIDE.md.

Survey context: FOLLOWUP.md Q2.
notes:
DONE 2026-05-26. Findings: .tasks/ClaudeConfig-40s.20-auto-mode-policy/FINDINGS.md; policy documented in docs/SANDBOX_GUIDE.md (new "Permission mode policy" section).

(a) autoMode.environment does the OPPOSITE of the hypothesis — it expands trusted infra, does NOT disable the .env-reading behavior. No knob disables that specific allow. Docs say deny rules are the only hard guarantee.

(b) Cleaner mechanism than hypothesized: permissions.disableAutoMode:"disable" in /etc/claude-code/managed-settings.json (purpose-built lock). Do NOT force permissionMode:"default" — that would also kill bypassPermissions/dontAsk which we want inside the sandbox. Backstops: auto is already ignored from project/local settings; Read(**/.env) deny rule (40s.16) neutralizes the behavior.

(c) Policy = prohibit auto (credential-classifier behavior + #97 autonomous sandbox-disable), permit bypassPermissions/dontAsk inside kernel sandbox (no credential behavior; UID+broker are the real boundary).

OPERATOR NOTE in findings: the auto-vs-bypass asymmetry is a deliberate design nuance — if a stricter stance is wanted (lock bypassPermissions host-wide too), it's a one-line managed-settings change.

Cross-bead: 40s.16 (Read(**/.env) deny is the hard guarantee), 40s.15 (emit disableAutoMode, NOT permissionMode force), DEC-013 (concrete mechanism for "auto prohibited" consequence).

title: Phase 6 design: per-worker subuid isolation for autosession swarm
description:
Per DEC-012 Phase 6 note: the autosession daemon spawns concurrent agent workers; if all share claude-session's UID, they can see each other's processes and files. Right mechanism is to allocate each worker a slice of claude-session's /etc/subuid range and launch in its own user namespace mapped to that slice.

This bead captures the design work: subuid range layout (e.g., claude-session:100000:1024 → 1024 workers possible, each getting 1 mapped UID), namespace bring-up via bwrap --userns or unshare --user, worker lifecycle (creation, exit cleanup, crash recovery), and interaction with DEC-013's egress mediation (does each worker get its own broker socket, or share claude-session's?).

Bonus topic for Firecracker primer: if VM-strength worker isolation is wanted (vs. user-namespace isolation), Firecracker microVMs are the natural answer — ~125ms boot, ~5MiB memory per VM, KVM-required. Compare and decide. Survey context: discussion thread 2026-05-26 around DEC-012 + DEC-013 + earlier Firecracker primer.
notes:
DONE 2026-05-26. Design doc: docs/design/phase6-worker-isolation.md (new docs/design/ subdir for pre-decisional internal architecture designs, with README).

Recommendation:
1. DEFAULT = Option A subuid user-namespaces. claude-session:100000:65536 in /etc/subuid; daemon launches each worker via bwrap --userns mapping worker N to host UID 100000+N. Kernel cross-UID DAC isolates siblings (ptrace/proc/files). Proportionate to the real threat (sibling reach-in), reuses bwrap (DEC-007/011), no hypervisor, WSL2-viable.
2. EGRESS = shared DEC-013 broker/proxy with per-worker UID attribution via SO_PEERCRED — NOT per-worker brokers (avoids multiplying the security-critical surface).
3. RESERVE Option B Firecracker microVMs for a future VM-strength requirement (untrusted multi-tenant). Disproportionate now: KVM dependency, VM image mgmt, WSL2 nested-KVM unreliable + interacts with the documented netns bug.

Marked PRE-DECISIONAL — binding choice lands in a DEC at Phase 6 kickoff. Open questions logged: subuid range width, per-worker scratch + free-list persistence, WSL2 userns reliability at swarm scale, interaction with the Phase 1.5 netns-confinement question (DEC-013 follow-up #1).

NOTE: created docs/design/ subdirectory — flagged for operator on whether to add a DEC formalizing it (per the directory-documentation convention).

title: Audit-logging hook suite: PreToolUse/PostToolUse → journald + per-session JSONL
description: Implement the audit-log architecture sketched in the survey: PreToolUse and PostToolUse hooks emit structured events to journald via systemd-cat (-t claude-session, -p info) for aggregator-friendly transport, plus a per-session JSONL trace at /home/claude-session/.cache/claude-config/<session-id>/tool-calls.jsonl for forensic detail. Compose with DEC-012's UID separation so 'journalctl _UID=claude-session' is the natural query interface. Reference FOLLOWUP.md Q5 for the OTEL-Collector-sidecar pattern that this can be a precursor to (BOCO telemetry routing reuses the same hook scaffold). Acceptance: a smoke-test session produces one journald entry per tool call AND one JSONL line per tool call in the cache dir.

Added dep on 40s.15; requeued to tail.

Adding a shared library extraction inline rather than deferring — the second hook is the natural refactor point; deferring past the git-guard family (8+ more hooks) would compound the diff risk. title: ConfigChange hook for marker-block tamper protection
description: Layer-2 protection on top of DEC-012's ACL-enforced marker-block boundary in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md. PreToolUse / ConfigChange hook detects mid-session modifications to settings files and either blocks them or alerts. ACLs are the preventative layer (kernel-enforced); this hook is the reactive layer (real-time detection). Both compose. Reference: FOLLOWUP.md Q5 OTEL pattern shows how to wire hooks. Acceptance: a session that attempts to edit ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md outside its marker block triggers a hook-emitted journald entry and (optionally) blocks the edit.
notes:
2026-05-26 (sandbox-wrapper queue): Deferred during autonomous iteration — depends on ClaudeConfig-40s.15. Acceptance requires a running sandboxed session, the hook wiring in the claude/ settings tree, and the installed marker block in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (all 40s.15 / installer). Reactive layer-2 over DEC-012's ACL boundary; handler design shares the journald scaffold with 40s.19. Added dep on 40s.15; requeued to tail.

PreToolUse contract (stdin {tool_name,tool_input.file_path,cwd}); emits hookSpecificOutput.permissionDecision allow|deny + reason; journald alert via systemd-cat -t claude-config-guard (stderr fallback). DENIES Write/Edit/MultiEdit/NotebookEdit to DEPLOYED config: ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, ~/.claude/settings.json, settings.local.json, ~/.claude.json, /etc/claude-code/*, ~/.claude/scripts/hooks/*. title: Evaluate PreToolUse hook for spawn-agents enforcement (if soft form drifts)
description:
The `spawn-agents` skill (filed today, deployed via chezmoi) and its CLAUDE.md trigger rule provide *soft* enforcement: the skill fires when the model deems it relevant, and the CLAUDE.md rule is a behavioral guideline. If the model forgets to consult the skill before an Agent call, the convention silently degrades.

A *harder* enforcement is possible: a `PreToolUse` hook on the `Agent` tool, configured in `~/.claude/settings.json`, that intercepts every Agent invocation and either:

- (a) emits a reminder/warning that the skill must be consulted (soft block — the user sees it, can choose to proceed)
- (b) blocks the invocation entirely until a sentinel marker is present (hard block — the model must explicitly indicate it's followed the procedure)
- (c) inspects the agent's prompt for hints about expected tools and surfaces a permission-decision prompt to the user before the agent runs (most invasive, most reliable)

## Acceptance

- After observing 5+ Agent tool calls across 5+ sessions, evaluate whether the soft form (skill + CLAUDE.md rule) is firing reliably.
- If yes: close this bead, document "soft enforcement sufficient" outcome.
- If no: design and prototype the hook (option a, b, or c based on what the failure modes look like).
- If hook is implemented: deploy via chezmoi as part of the global settings.json hooks block; commit; document in spawn-agents SKILL.md.

## Open

- The `update-config` skill is the canonical home for hooks-related changes; the hook implementation should compose with that skill.
- Whether the hook can read the Agent tool's `prompt` parameter at PreToolUse time (and use it for option c) — needs verification by reading hook docs.
- Whether the hook fires for ALL Agent invocations or only top-level ones (sub-agents spawning sub-sub-agents — relevant for autosession daemon design in Phase 6).

## Background

Filed 2026-05-05 as a follow-up to the spawn-agents skill design discussion. The hook is a backup plan; primary intent is for the soft form to suffice. This bead exists so the question doesn't get lost if the soft form drifts.

title: Design Phase 7 executor as multi-backend dispatcher (E2B / AIO / K8s candidates)
description:
Surfaced by ClaudeConfig-40s.12 (sandbox-tooling survey, 2026-05-05). Design the Phase 7 privileged-action executor as a multi-backend dispatcher rather than building sandbox-spawning ourselves for capabilities that just need "execute untrusted code."

## The reframing

Phase 7 (per DEC-003) was originally framed as "build a mediator daemon that *executes* privileged actions." The survey suggests an alternative framing: **build the mediator as a *dispatcher* with pluggable execution backends.**

Specific backend candidates per capability class:

- **Untrusted code execution** (Jupyter, build scripts, data transforms): dispatch to E2B (or self-hosted Firecracker analog). Apache-2.0 OSS infra exists; SDKs in Python/TS.
- **Browser / GUI / computer-use automation**: dispatch to agent-infra/sandbox (AIO Sandbox). Bundled Chrome+VNC+code-server is fit-for-purpose. Note: weaker isolation (Docker + relaxed seccomp); only suitable for capabilities where the backend's threat surface is acceptable.
- **K8s pod scheduling** (only if user runs a cluster — not our user, but relevant for shareable-tool future scope): dispatch to a kubernetes-sigs/agent-sandbox `SandboxClaim`.

## Why this matters

Reduces Phase 7 implementation surface meaningfully. Writing a hardened-runtime spawner is multi-week work; integrating with E2B's SDK is days. The mediator daemon, capability manifest, audit log, and request schema are still our work — those are the high-trust components — but the actual sandbox-spawning becomes a backend driver per capability class.

Also: when Phase 7 design begins, DEC-003 will be replaced with a non-provisional decision entry. The dispatcher framing should land in that revision.

## Acceptance

- DEC-003's replacement (filed at Phase 7 implementation start) records the dispatcher design with named candidate backends.
- A capability manifest schema is defined that includes a `backend:` field per capability.
- At least one backend driver is implemented (E2B is the lightest first target).
- Survey artifacts referenced: .tasks/40s.12-sandbox-tooling-survey/SUMMARY.md.

## Open

- Self-hosted Firecracker (without E2B SaaS) feasibility for single-workstation deployment — worth a prototype scan when Phase 7 begins.
- BOCO's procurement / IT stance on E2B SaaS as a developer-tool dependency.

title: Jira/Atlassian service account for claude-session (with IT)
description:
Pursue a dedicated Atlassian service account from BOCO IT for `claude-session` to use, replacing the interim personal-token arrangement (sibling bead).

## Why this matters

A personal token (interim arrangement) inherits the issuing user's full Jira permissions. A service account can be:
- Scoped to specific Jira projects (BOCO etc.) via group membership
- Read-only at the principal level, not just per-token
- Audited as a non-human entity at the IT level

This addresses the Q&A point that "Atlassian's API token model doesn't natively scope to specific projects (it's user-bound, all-or-nothing within that user's permissions). The way to get scope discipline is the user the token represents."

## Acceptance

- IT-issued service account, e.g., `claude-session@boldorange.com` or similar
- Group membership scoped to specific Jira projects (BOCO + others Claude works in)
- API token issued to that service account, transferred to claude-session via the standard tier-1 provisioning path (no escape hatch needed; the principal is already separate)
- DEC-011 amended (or DEC-012 added) to reflect the service-account version superseding the interim personal-token arrangement
- Interim Jira bead closed

## Open

- BOCO IT's policy on service accounts for AI tooling. Worth a documentation/policy ask alongside the request — sets precedent for future agent-tooling credentials.
notes: Note 2026-05-05: DEC numbering predictions in the original description (DEC-011/DEC-012) were based on assumption that DEC-010 would be the NR record. DEC-010 was filed as the escape-hatch policy instead. Specific DEC numbers for the service-account credential will be assigned when provisioned.
