title: [spike] Vault PKI engine for per-session CA management
description:
Research spike, conditional on ClaudeConfig-jgp (per-session CA TLS-terminating proxy) being adopted. If we ever do per-session CA generation + rotation, Vault's PKI engine is the natural backend: handles CA hierarchy, signs leaf certs on demand, rotates roots, audit logs.

Operationally distinct from the swarm-credentials Vault spike (ClaudeConfig-9v8) — different engine (PKI vs KV/dynamic-creds), different use case. Could share a single Vault deployment if both ever materialize.

Status: idea park. Do not work until ClaudeConfig-jgp progresses.

title: [spike] Per-session ephemeral CA TLS-terminating proxy (forced by ECH adoption)
description:
Research spike. Currently rejected by DEC-013 in favor of the SNI-inspecting (non-terminating) proxy — CA-cert-as-sandbox-trust-root is too expensive a trust footprint. If/when ECH (Encrypted Client Hello) adoption forces the issue (years out per current trajectory — see ClaudeConfig-ciw.6), revisit with the mitigations discussed in the 2026-06-02 design discussion:

Mitigations to evaluate:
- Per-session ephemeral CA: generate a fresh CA at session start, bind it into the sandbox's trust store only for that session's lifetime, destroy after.
- Periodic rotation timer: scheduled regeneration of CA roots even within long-lived sessions.
- Per-session identity at the proxy: SO_PEERCRED on the broker socket works for UDS; for TCP-to-proxy, need per-session netns (ClaudeConfig-ciw.5) or per-session port range to identify which CA to use.
- Vault PKI engine for CA management (see follow-up spike).

Open questions:
- Does the proxy plaintext-in-memory concern survive these mitigations? (Best assessment: yes — ephemeral CAs reduce blast radius across sessions but don't reduce the proxy's steady-state value as a target.)
- Is the operational complexity justified vs accepting reduced destination-control for uncredentialed traffic once SNI is no longer plaintext?

Status: idea park. Do not work until ECH measurably breaks SNI proxying (track via ClaudeConfig-ciw.6).

title: [spike] Evaluate Vault / OpenBao for swarm credential management
description:
Research spike. Phase 1.5 ships with pass(1) under /home/claude-egress/.password-store as the broker's credential backend. When/if swarm functionality (autonomous multi-session pools) matures past prototype, re-evaluate whether a Vault-shaped secret manager becomes worthwhile.

Trigger conditions for revisiting:
- Per-session credential scoping at swarm scale (>10 concurrent sessions).
- Need for dynamic credentials the issuing service can mint (DB users, AWS STS, ephemeral PostgreSQL for sandboxed tests, etc.) — Vault's PKI/database engines shine here.
- Multi-host or multi-team deployment.
- Audit-trail requirements that pass's filesystem layout can't satisfy.

If/when triggered: compare HashiCorp Vault Community (BSL-licensed) against OpenBao (MPL fork, IBM/Snowflake-backed) and infisical (newer, OSS daemon).

Status: idea park. Do not work until a swarm bead surfaces the forcing function.

title: git-guard C9: docs + retire redundant git allow-rules
description:
Final. Depends on C1-C8 (the hook must be trusted before pruning allow-rules). Source: ~/Source/claude-config-handoff.md §9 (Child 9); epic ClaudeConfig-bi0.

Document git-guard.yaml (global + project schemas) and the behavior/decision model (safe-verb fast-allow; protected-branch ask; ref-rewriting denylist; branch naming; git -C global-ask/project-allow; most-restrictive-wins composition; guardrail-not-sandbox framing §8). Once the hook is trusted, PRUNE now-redundant coarse git allow-rules from settings so the hook is the actual decision-maker rather than being short-circuited by a pre-existing allow.

CURATION/OVERLAP: this targets the existing Bash(git:*) allow-rule (and any broad Bash(git -C * ...) entries) in .claude/settings.local.json / global settings. Coordinate with the broader permissions theme; do not remove an allow-rule until the hook demonstrably covers its cases.
notes: DEC-017 (2026-05-27): implementation language switched from Python to Lua. See ClaudeConfig-bi0 epic note. Blocked on ClaudeConfig-58n.1 (LuaJIT + lyaml + lua-cjson provisioning).

title: Track ECH adoption; plan SNI-proxy sunset
description: DEC-013 follow-up #3. When Encrypted Client Hello is widely deployed, the SNI proxy's inspection fails (SNI no longer plaintext). Track ECH adoption; plan migration to DNS-allowlist + nftables IP rules when it crosses a threshold.

title: Interactive notification button → claude session round-trip
description:
Extend the per-session notification system with **interactive button-press round-trip**: when a Claude session emits a Notification toast with action buttons (e.g., "approve" / "reject" / "defer"), pressing a button delivers the response back to the running Claude session.

## Three-layer architecture

| Layer | Status | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| 1: Toast button → Windows action | Already supported by BurntToast | `New-BTButton -Content $label -Arguments $action` |
| 2: Windows action → WSL2 script | Standard | Custom protocol handler registered in Windows registry, mapping e.g. `claude-action://session-id/approve` to `wsl -e ~/.claude/scripts/respond-to-prompt session-id approve` |
| 3: WSL2 script → running Claude session's prompt | The hard part — needs investigation | See "Mechanism options" below |

## Mechanism options for Layer 3

The fundamental constraint: Claude Code is a TUI; permission prompts read from a controlling terminal. There's no API for "approve the current prompt programmatically." The script needs to inject input into the terminal Claude is reading from.

- **kitty remote control (`kitten @ send-text`).** Kitty (the terminal emulator currently in use) supports remote-control protocols that can inject text into a window/pane. If claude runs in a kitty window, this works without any new shell layer. **Probably the lightest path for current workflow.**
- **Pty injection via `/proc/<pid>/fd/0`.** Direct write to the claude process's stdin fd. Works on Linux but is brittle: requires the controlling tty to permit it; can corrupt session state if mistimed; some kernels disable it via `dev.tty.legacy_tiocsti_restrict`.
- **tmux/screen/zellij send-keys.** Standard for terminal multiplexer users. The user doesn't currently run tmux but would consider adoption if the productivity feature is meaningful enough to justify it.
- **A wrapper that adopts a pty.** Run `claude` under a pty wrapper (e.g., `script(1)` or a custom Python/Go wrapper using `pty.fork`); the wrapper exposes a side-channel for input injection. Heaviest; reserved if simpler options fail.

The investigation should evaluate these against: (a) does it work for the user's actual setup, (b) how invasive is the setup, (c) failure modes, (d) does it compose with the eventual claude-sandbox wrapper (Phase 1+).

## Implementation sketch

- Custom Windows protocol `claude-action://` registered via .reg file delivered through Chezmoi (one-time setup; documented in repo).
- `~/.claude/scripts/hooks/respond-to-prompt`: takes session-id + action, identifies the right injection target (per the chosen Layer-3 mechanism), delivers the keystroke that maps to the action (e.g., "approve" → "1\n" for the typical "1. Yes" prompt response).
- `claude-notify` helper grows a `--interactive` flag that adds default action buttons to the toast with appropriate `claude-action://` URIs as button arguments.

## Caveats

- Only works when claude runs inside whichever terminal/multiplexer the chosen Layer-3 mechanism targets. Outside that, button presses are no-ops (or fall back to a "respond manually" toast).
- Mapping action names to keystroke responses depends on Claude Code's prompt UI conventions remaining stable. If those change, the mapping breaks.
- Custom protocol registration is per-Windows-user; need a Chezmoi script to manage the .reg file.
- If the chosen mechanism requires adopting a new tool (e.g., tmux), that's a workflow change worth evaluating on its own merits — not just for this feature.

## Acceptance

- Notification toast with buttons appears.
- Pressing a button delivers the action to the claude session via the chosen Layer-3 mechanism.
- Session sees the equivalent of the user typing the corresponding response key + Enter.
- Works for the common cases: approve/reject permission prompts, "yes" to a "would you like to..." question.
- Documented integration: how to enable, what it does, what to do if it breaks.
- Layer-3 mechanism choice recorded in DECISION_LOG (with the alternatives considered and the verdict).

## Out of scope

- Custom button-action mapping per-prompt (just the standard approve/reject/etc. for now).
- Mobile push notifications, email integrations, or non-Windows toast paths.

## Background

Filed 2026-05-06 as a follow-up to the per-session notification system design discussion. The basic notification system (Layers 1+2 — toast appears, buttons present but inert) ships first; this bead captures the Layer-3 round-trip feature for later.

title: bd formula for three-phase tooling-survey workflow
description:
# bd formula for three-phase tooling-survey workflow

## What

The 2026-04-22 beads-adjacent tooling survey (`docs/reviews/beads-tooling-survey-2026-04-22.md`) used a specific three-phase shape:

1. **Phase 1 — triage.** One Sonnet agent reads all READMEs; produces a 3-line triage card per tool; halts for user review.
2. **Phase 2 — deep dives.** Parallel Opus agents (one per green-lit tool) produce per-tool dossiers at `docs/reviews/<survey-slug>/dossier-<tool>.md`. Each gated on explicit user green-light from Phase 1.
3. **Phase 3 — synthesis.** Main-context aggregation into a top-level verdict report with bottom-line verdicts, cross-cutting findings, and prioritized cherry-picks.

This shape is reusable. The next survey (tools, libraries, architectural options, vendor evaluations) will want the same scaffolding. This bead encodes it as a bd formula: one `bd mol pour tooling-survey --var slug=<topic> --var tools=<list>` spawns the anchor epic, per-tool Phase 1 triage tasks, placeholders for Phase 2 dossiers (gated open after user green-light), and a Phase 3 synthesis task with dependency edges to each dossier.

## Why

- Repeatable shape; the three-phase halt-for-review gate is non-trivial scaffolding that benefits from being encoded.
- Composition check: does `bd formula` support conditional / gated step activation, or are Phase 2 dossiers spawned eagerly and closed as `wontfix` for tools that don't get green-lit? Answering this for this shape informs what `bd formula` can and can't do for other workflows.
- Second canonical exemplar for the epic — the branching-on-user-decision axis that plugin-doc doesn't exercise.

## Shape sketch

```yaml
name: tooling-survey
variables:
  slug: {required: true}
  tools: {required: true, type: list}
steps:
  - id: anchor
    type: epic
    title: "Tooling survey: {{slug}}"
    labels: [area:research, scope:local]
  - id: "triage-{{each tools}}"
    type: task
    title: "Phase 1 triage: {{item}}"
    parent: anchor
    # ... spawned once per tool
  - id: synthesis
    type: task
    title: "Phase 3 synthesis"
    parent: anchor
    depends_on: [triage-*]
    # Phase 2 dossier tasks created ad-hoc post-green-light OR spawned eagerly and closed on no-go
```

The exact shape depends on what the schema supports (discovered in blocking bead).

## Deliverables

- `.beads/formulas/tooling-survey.{json|yaml|toml}`
- `.beads/formulas/tooling-survey.README.md` — includes guidance on how to handle Phase 1 halt/green-light (explicit design decision based on what formulas can and can't express).
- Brief decision note on branching-step support added to `memory/reference-bd-capabilities.md` § "bd formula" (augmenting the section established by the schema-discovery bead).

## Verify

- `bd cook tooling-survey --var slug=test --var tools='["a","b","c"]' --dry-run` produces expected step expansion.
- Replay: re-run the beads-tooling-survey against a small hypothetical tool list and confirm the spawned issue tree matches the hand-rolled 2026-04-22 structure.

## Dependencies

**Blocked by:** plugin-doc/worker-doc formula bead (shares schema-discovery cost; no reason to solve it twice).

**Sibling (no dep):** incident-postmortem formula — different complexity axis (severity-branching), can proceed in parallel once schema is known.

## Provenance

Child of epic `<formula-epic-id>`. Discovered from cross-cutting finding of `docs/reviews/beads-tooling-survey-2026-04-22.md` — the survey's three-phase shape is itself a formula candidate per recommendation #5 in the post-probe design conversation.

## Estimate

`est-h:2 est-p:1 est-t:S` — contingent on schema-discovery being complete before start; if the schema supports conditional gating naturally, lower; if we need a halt-and-manually-gate workaround, slightly higher.

comments.author: Claude Code
comments.text: Scaffolded by Claude Code 2026-04-22. Exercises the multi-tool-expansion + halt-for-review-gate axis of bd formulas. Blocked by .9.1 (shared schema cost). P4 because next-survey demand is speculative.

title: (EPIC) Shareable Claude config repo — installable tooling starter kit
description:
Standalone repo of Claude-related skills, slash commands, agents, scripts, and scheduler/daemon configuration, designed to be installable and adaptable by technical friends and colleagues (inside and outside BOCO). Payload comes from the J121-9kp initiative; this epic covers packaging, distribution, and self-documentation.

**Motivation.** Multiple friends and coworkers have asked to adopt this workflow. The current Chezmoi-managed source is Chezmoi-convention-laden and not ideal for sharing — the shareable repo should treat Chezmoi as an implementation detail and rewrite installation as a standalone process.

**Scope.**
- `README.md`: installation, component breakdown, workflow explanation, explicit source attribution (e.g., Superpowers cherries)
- One-shot install scripts for Linux/WSL and Windows. These probably comprise most of the effort: discover machine-local values (hostname, home, paths), prompt for preferences (where `/idea` writes, whether `/reminder` uses cron or systemd, notification daemon choice), substitute into target markdown/script files, install recommended Claude plugins (claude-mem and others)
- Example conversation transcripts illustrating tooling usage
- Test prompts users can run (point Claude at) to verify installed tooling
- `/tutor` slash command for self-documentation: `/tutor /<command>` explains purpose and calling syntax; flags: `--short`/`--long`, `--provenance`, `--history`, `--example[s]`

**Implementation phases.**
1. **Audit + scrub.** Credentials, `pass` paths, BOCO email, Atlassian cloud ID, account IDs, machine names. Explicit audit pass, not a grep. The quiet tax nobody budgets for.
2. **Rewrite-as-standalone.** Chezmoi-free layout. What's shareable is the tools and patterns, not the dotfile conventions.
3. **Linux/WSL installer.** The easier of the two.
4. **Windows installer.** Different enough from Linux to be its own phase: PowerShell, BurntToast, Task Scheduler vs. systemd.
5. **Example transcripts + test prompts.**
6. **`/tutor` command.**

**Tradeoffs to resolve before starting (both shape install-script design).**
- **Starter-kit vs. living-project.** Fork-and-go (low maintenance) vs. pull-updates (higher value, commits you to semver / changelogs / breaking-change discipline). Decide upfront.
- **Divergence expectation.** Once shared, users will customize. How much upstream fidelity should updates assume?

**Scoping decisions captured 2026-04-20 (also in `bd memories claude-config-repo`).**
- This is a separate top-level initiative from J121-9kp, not a wave within it. J121-9kp produces the payload; this repo packages and distributes. Conflating them forces install-script maintenance against a moving target.
- `/tutor` belongs here, not J121-9kp Wave 2. In isolation it overlaps `/docs`; bundled with this repo it earns its keep as discoverability for users who didn't build the tools themselves. The `--provenance` flag also forces a useful discipline: per-tool `source:`/`decided:` frontmatter.

**When this work starts: export everything to a new repo/directory.**
- Do not continue planning in J121's auto-memory or `tasks/` directories. This is not J121 work.
- Existing scratchpad `claude-config-repo.idea.md` at J121 root (untracked) — migrate to the new repo's `docs/` or `deliverables/` on day one, then delete from J121.
- Decompose this epic into child beads in a **new beads workspace** (likely the new repo's `.beads/`), not J121's tracker. J121's `bd` should carry the epic as a placeholder reference only; real work items live in the new workspace.

**Blocked on:** J121-9kp (parent of Waves 1-3) — let the payload stabilize before packaging.
design:
Repo structure (tentative, decide at kickoff):
  /README.md                       — top-level install + workflow explanation
  /install/linux-wsl.sh            — Linux + WSL installer
  /install/windows.ps1             — Windows installer
  /skills/                         — SKILL.md files (vendored + original)
  /commands/                       — slash command markdown
  /agents/                         — agent definitions
  /scripts/                        — bd-timew, reload-systemd-user, etc.
  /systemd/                        — user units + timers (Linux/WSL)
  /scheduler/                      — Windows Task Scheduler equivalents
  /examples/transcripts/           — example conversation transcripts
  /examples/test-prompts/          — runnable verification prompts
  /docs/                           — workflow guide, provenance, migration notes

Install-time decisions to prompt for (not exhaustive):
- Notification daemon (BurntToast, notify-send, wsl-notify-send, none)
- Reminder backend (cron, systemd timer, Windows Task Scheduler)
- /idea destination (directory or git repo path)
- Claude plugins to install (claude-mem, others)
- Machine name / hostname (auto-detected, confirm)
- Home directory path (auto-detected, confirm)
notes: Captured 2026-04-20 as a deferred idea; not to be started until J121-9kp Waves 1-3 are substantially complete. See bd memories for scoping decisions.

value: Decision 2026-04-20 (mhaynes): bv (beads_viewer, github.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_viewer) upstream LICENSE is MIT-with-rider that explicitly denies rights to OpenAI, Anthropic, their Affiliates, and 'any person or entity acting directly or indirectly on behalf of' them — including 'copying, modifying, merging, ..., executing, benchmarking, testing, analyzing, indexing, or incorporating the Software into any ... evaluation harness, or pipeline for machine learning or other automated systems.' Claude Code invoking 'bv --robot-*' on behalf of the user plausibly falls under the 'acting on behalf of' clause, and --robot-* flags are designed for AI consumption, making the 'pipeline for automated systems' clause directly on-point. User verified (with Claude assist) that beads_viewer is NOT listed in github.com/jeremylongshore/claude-code-plugins-plus-skills despite author having OTHER unrelated plugins there — marketplace aggregation would not override upstream license anyway. Risk accepted for personal-tooling use (scope:local, client:personal, case:area:claude) based on: (a) user is BOCO employee not an Anthropic affiliate; (b) 'acting on behalf of' clause's enforceability against end-users of a Claude Code client is genuinely ambiguous; (c) personal-tooling use is not commercial redistribution. Risk NOT cleared for: (a) team-wide or BOCO-facing adoption; (b) incorporation into billable deliverables; (c) public redistribution of derivative tooling. Integration surfaces that invoke --robot-*: /next (delegated to --robot-next via J121 bead-for-refactor), /triage (planned, new bead J121-tbd), /session-checkpoint enrichment (planned). If license stance changes or upstream rider softens, revisit integrations.

branching-strategy-2026-06-02: Project CLAUDE.md branching rules updated (commit f2b8b1b). Three principles now codified: (1) Agents MAY operate on main directly for merges, hotfixes, beads-state commits, and bookkeeping — feature-branch-by-default applies to NEW feature work only, not as a hard prohibition. (2) NO ORPHAN feature branches: any implementation work on a feature branch must either merge to main before session end, OR be tracked by an explicit review/merge bead that BLOCKS the next dependent implementation bead. Rationale: bd's ready queue is the authoritative 'what can I work on' surface; an orphan branch is invisible to bd, and the next session will branch off a main missing prerequisites it has no way to discover. (3) Prefer short branch lifetimes (merge before session end) over the review-bead pattern; reserve review beads for branches that genuinely need to outlive their session (incubation, external review, large WIP). Concrete near-miss that prompted the rule: ClaudeConfig-ciw.2 depended on ciw.1's branch code, but ciw.1 was closed in bd while its branch was unmerged — caught at /clear-time, would have stranded the next session.

Decision 2026-04-20 (confirmed): editorMode=vim must live in ~/.claude.json, NOT ~/.claude/settings.json. User tested settings.json placement and Claude Code ignored it. Fix deployed via chezmoi 'modify' script at ~/.local/share/chezmoi/home/modify_private_dot_claude.json — jq pipeline that surgically sets .editorMode without chezmoi owning the full file. Prefix order matters: modify_private_ (not private_modify_) — with private_ first, chezmoi consumes only the private_ attribute and treats 'modify_dot_claude.json' as a literal filename. Script is defensive: passes stdin through unchanged if jq missing; exits 0 on empty stdin (target doesn't exist) to avoid stubbing a file Claude Code might overwrite without merging. Executable bit not needed on source (chezmoi copies contents to temp file and sets bit). private_ attribute preserves the 0600 mode on ~/.claude.json (holds OAuth tokens). Claude Code picks up editorMode changes live — no restart required (confirmed 2026-04-20). Rejected full-file chezmoi-management: 47KB of 64KB churn-heavy state (projects, counters, timestamps) that would conflict across machines and leak oauthAccount in a git remote.

just is exclusively for Categories A+B (claude-config dev/build/management tasks). mise is exclusively for Category D (session scaffolding) as a coordinator implementation detail, NOT a recommendation to user projects. Category C (user's project) is tool-agnostic — claude-config does not care what the project uses. mise installed into claude-session's home (DEC-016); coordinator generates mise config inside bind-mounted worktree; user's project can use anything (mise/just/make/Taskfile/devbox/Nix/npm). expose_user_mise allowlist controls Category C → D crossing.

bv extended robot flags (beyond --robot-triage used in /next): --robot-next (single top pick + claim command), --robot-plan (parallel execution tracks with unblocks lists), --robot-insights (full graph metrics: PageRank, betweenness, HITS, eigenvector, k-core, critical path), --robot-alerts (stale issues, blocking cascades, priority mismatches), --robot-suggest (hygiene: duplicate detection, missing deps, label suggestions, cycle breaks), --robot-diff --diff-since <ref> (changes since a git ref: new/closed/modified), --robot-graph --graph-format json|dot|mermaid (dependency graph export)

lua-hook-bytecode-cache-deferred: pre-compiling _lib.lua via luajit -b would save ~0.5-1ms per hook invocation by skipping parse. Not pursued now — premature when hook count is 2. Reconsider if (a) hook count reaches 5+ AND (b) aggregate require time becomes measurable in 100-call sessions (>200ms total).

lua-hook-not-extracted: PROTECTED_PATHS/PROTECTED_PREFIXES (config-guard's threat model), build_record/journal_line (audit-event's record shape), WRITE_TOOLS (config-guard's tool matcher). These are hook-specific domain logic, not generic utilities — keep inline in each hook.

Category C → D exposure: top-level exposeUserMise section with per-section allowlist (tools/env/tasks/hooks). Default: tools=true (safe — toolchain versions); all others false (env can carry secrets/assumptions; tasks/hooks may have host-side coupling). mise's native multi-config-file merge model handles layering: user's project .mise.toml is bind-mounted into worktree at expected path, coordinator-generated config layered on top with leaf-level override semantics. Users wanting more sharing flip individual flags; users wanting none keep all false and re-declare tools they need in claude-session.yaml.

bd list --json appends a human-readable 'Showing N issues...' footer AFTER the JSON array when results are truncated by --limit (default 50). This breaks strict JSON parsers. Workaround: pipe through `sed -n '1,/^]$/p'` to cut at the closing bracket line. bd ready --json does NOT have this bug. Affects: /show slash command (J121-9kp.2.15), any future tooling that consumes bd list --json output.

beads-embedded-mode-performance: Embedded Dolt mode opens and closes the DB engine on every bd invocation (no persistent process). Two compounding slowness classes: (1) Cold page-cache reads — after WSL2 restart or cache eviction, all .beads/embeddeddolt/ reads hit the VHD directly; first commands are 5-30s even with a small journal. Warm-up is automatic after a few reads. (2) Journal regrowth between cleanups — every bd write (even in batch mode) appends to the chunk journal; with a daily cleanup window you can accumulate 24h of writes before the next gc, causing reads to slow again mid-session. Fix for (2): run bd-timew cleanup on-demand or increase cleanup frequency. Fix for both: switch to server mode (bd init --server) — the dolt sql-server keeps its buffer pool warm in memory, eliminating cold-read overhead and keeping read latency near 0 regardless of journal state. Server mode requires the dolt binary (not bundled with bd); install via chezmoi run_onchange_install-dolt.sh.tmpl.

lua-hook-shared-lib-pattern: hooks under claude/scripts/hooks/ require a sibling _lib.lua module for shared utilities (normalize_path, match_paths, journal, emit_hook_output, append_jsonl, now_iso, parse_kv_args, is_main). Wrapper env-scrub sets LUA_PATH=${SHARE_DIR}/hooks/?.lua;... so require resolves both at install path and in the assembled claude-session home. Extraction happened at hook #2 (audit-event); cost ~1-2ms first-require per hook process, acceptable against the ~50ms Python baseline DEC-017 displaced.

rebase-landmine-beads-jsonl-only-commits: Standalone 'chore(beads): export state' commits (whose ONLY content is .beads/issues.jsonl) cause unresolvable conflicts when rebasing one feature branch onto another, because both branches export the same auto-managed file independently. Resolution: 'git rebase --skip' the JSONL-only commit (main usually has fresher state from the other branch's beads-export-via-pre-commit-hook). Prevention: don't create separate chore(beads) commits — the pre-commit hook auto-stages .beads/issues.jsonl alongside the actual feature changes, so they ride together as one commit.

autosession-memory-format: bd remember entries added by the autosession coordinator must start with 'autosession[<slug>]:' and end with a pointer to the session's exit-summary.md for audit provenance. Example format: 'autosession[tooling-wave-1-3-20260421]: <fact> (see tasks/sessions/tooling-wave-1-3-20260421/exit-summary.md#discoveries)'. The prefix lets 'bd memories autosession' surface the cohort and 'bd memories autosession[<slug>]' narrow to one session. The pointer lets /import-session reviewers drill back to context without guessing. Gated by lifecycle.memory.can_remember in the beads template.

dolt-sql-server-exclusive-write-lock: Two dolt sql-server processes cannot share the same data directory. Dolt acquires an exclusive write lock on the .dolt/noms/ directory. Attempting to start a second server against .beads/dolt while the primary (port 41009) is running fails immediately with 'database is locked by another dolt process'. Physical clone (Tier 3) is required for a truly isolated second server. See tasks/J121-9kp-2-17-5/dolt-sandbox-tier2-findings.md.

bd-ready-sort-hybrid-not-useful: bd ready --sort hybrid surfaces stale P4 backlog items above P0 critical work (tested 2026-04-27). At top of hybrid list: J121-850 P4, J121-7w0 P4, vs priority sort showing J121-qu4/k7f/ao6/hae P0 first. Hybrid sort appears age-weighted in a way that punishes recently-filed high-priority items. Use --sort priority (default) for daily /next flow. Hybrid is unsuitable for J121 workflow.

svc-labels-unused: No beads currently have svc: labels even though the label taxonomy supports them and bd-timew.yaml maps area: labels to billing tuples. User flagged this 2026-04-21 as a gap to fix. When adding svc: labels, the value should match the Svc field from bd-timew start output (e.g., svc:Technology-Services). Start by adding to all billable beads under BOCO-18077 and BOCO-17078.

bd-custom-status-review-semantics: Custom status 'review' (status.custom=review) uses activity tier [unspecified], which means: (1) bd ready EXCLUDES review beads (correct — keeps them out of the work queue); (2) bd list INCLUDES them in default --status open filter (correct — discoverable); (3) bd close ALWAYS lands on 'closed', not 'review' — to send to review use 'bd update --status review'; (4) to return from review to active work use 'bd update --status open' or '--status in_progress'. This is the right semantic for a human-review queue gate.

bd-formula-not-for-runtime-policy: bd formulas describe issue-DAG spawn templates (variables, steps, parent/child edges) cooked into protos and poured into real beads. They do NOT constrain runtime coordinator behavior on existing beads — allowed status transitions, note prefixes, label can/can't-touch, metadata rewrite timing all live outside the formula layer. When a policy is ~80% runtime behavior and ~20% create-time, ship YAML-read-by-coordinator and skip the formula conversion; partial conversion splits the policy across two mental models without payoff. Re-evaluate if bd grows runtime-policy primitives (e.g., a bd gate shape blocking out-of-allowlist transitions). Verified 2026-04-22 via J121-7bv.1 probe: bd formula list ships zero bundled examples, schema is documented only via --help, formulas failed validation on both flat and wrapped JSON attempts.

bd-timew-server-mode-user-provisioning-gap: bd dolt set user and bd-timew init-project --server only write client-side config (metadata.json, pass entry, .envrc BEADS_DOLT_PASSWORD); they do NOT create the user in Dolt's mysql.user table. Without explicit provisioning, bd commands fail with 'Access denied for user' on the first connection after a fresh server-mode init. Fixed in J121-ai4 by adding _provision_dolt_user helper that runs CREATE USER IF NOT EXISTS + ALTER USER + GRANT for localhost/127.0.0.1/%% host variants via mysql -u root after _provision_pass_entry.

Decision 2026-04-20: the shareable Claude config repo is a SEPARATE top-level initiative (J121-014), not a wave inside J121-9kp. Rationale: J121-9kp produces the payload (skills, commands, agents, scripts); the config repo packages and distributes them. Conflating the two would force install-script maintenance against a moving target. Implementation must not start until J121-9kp Waves 1-3 are substantially complete, and when it does start it must export all planning context/artifacts to the new repo/directory (not J121's auto-memory or tasks/).

bd-lint-integration-decision: bd lint should NOT be added to bd-timew cleanup sweep or run on a timer. It found 105/108 existing issues failing (missing description sections) — all pre-dating the description-quality convention. Running on a timer would be noise-only. Correct usage: ad-hoc, manually, when creating a batch of new issues. bd-timew cleanup should remain: compact + gc + stale check. bd lint stays as a standalone command the user invokes when needed. See tasks/J121-7bv/deliverables/beads-feature-survey.md section 6.

beads-dolt-env-vars: Correct BEADS_DOLT_* env var names (confirmed from bd binary strings, v1.0.2): BEADS_DOLT_SERVER_USER (not BEADS_DOLT_USER), BEADS_DOLT_SERVER_DATABASE (not BEADS_DOLT_DATABASE), BEADS_DOLT_SERVER_HOST, BEADS_DOLT_SERVER_PORT, BEADS_DOLT_PASSWORD, BEADS_DOLT_AUTO_START, BEADS_DOLT_MAX_CONNS, BEADS_DOLT_SHARED_SERVER, BEADS_DOLT_READY_TIMEOUT, BEADS_DOLT_LOG_MAX_BYTES. BEADS_DOLT_SERVER_DATABASE rejects slashes — cannot specify db/branch format (bd validates against [a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*).

Slash command frontmatter conventions (apply whenever editing any ~/.claude/commands/*.md or project .claude/commands/*.md file): (1) argument-hint field must accurately reflect all accepted flags after every edit — update it whenever flags are added, removed, or renamed; (2) timestamps array is append-only: add a new entry {action: updated, at: <ISO-8601>, actor: <git user.name>, note: <one-line summary of change>} for every edit; (3) comments array: add a new bullet for substantive changes (new flag families, behavioral shifts); mechanical fixes go in timestamps[].note only — do not duplicate in comments. These conventions are enforced by /edit-command step 7 invariants.

Title convention for src:jira beads: always prepend the Jira key as 'BOCO-XXXXX · <original title>'. Example: 'BOCO-18077 · Great Clips | Pipeline Review/Validation'. Apply when creating via bd create or when a jira sync imports a new ticket. Rationale: bd list and bd show surface the Jira key immediately without needing to inspect external_ref. Applied retroactively to all src:jira beads on 2026-04-21.

Estimate label convention: three dimensions — est-h:N (hours, only dimension bd-timew variance reports consult), est-p:N (story points, scrum), est-t:S (t-shirt size: XS/S/M/L/XL, scrum). Issues may carry any subset. Apply at creation time; retrofit only when touching issues for other reasons. Hours use whole/half integers (est-h:0.5 min). Query via bd list --label-pattern='est-*:*' or --label-pattern='est-h:*'. Full convention + rationale in auto-memory feedback-time-tracking.md. Decided 2026-04-19.

etckeeper-commit-protocol: This host's etckeeper repo (origin: git@github.com:AudensAurantius/etckeeper) is git-crypt-armed with a 40check-secrets pre-commit hook. Sensitive files (shadow/gshadow/ssl/private/mysql/debian.cnf/docker-key/exim-creds/mail-tls) are git-crypt-encrypted via /etc/.gitattributes; the hook blocks any root-only tracked file that's neither encrypted nor in /etc/etckeeper/git-crypt-allowlist. Future sessions must run `sudo etckeeper commit '...'` after any /etc change, NEVER bypass the gate (--no-verify etc), and treat any block as a real failure: either add the file to .gitattributes with 'filter=git-crypt diff=git-crypt' BEFORE first commit if secret, or add to git-crypt-allowlist if root-only-but-non-secret. World-readable files (passwd, group) are not gated and don't need either treatment. Audit before commit: `sudo bash -c 'cd /etc && git status'` to see staged paths; `sudo bash -c 'cd /etc && git check-attr filter <path>'` to verify a path's encryption status.

Sidecar config: <project>/.claude/claude-session/config.yaml (nested dir lets custom scripts/templates coexist alongside config). Format-agnostic: YAML | JSON | Cue all accepted; Cue canonical for schema (extends DEC-024). All keys camelCase (ADO precedent). Top-level sections: env, tools, git, hooks (gitGuard subsection), shellHooks, exposeUserMise, beads (TBD per bd-timew merge), setup (env/tools/git declarative + steps procedural), teardown (narrow declarative whitelist: beads + git + steps). Hook configs fold IN: e.g. hooks.gitGuard.allowedGitDirs replaces the prior separate <project>/.claude-session/hooks/git-guard.yaml file. git-guard.lua's load_project_config() needs a migration to read the new key name + path.

Sidecar config design: tasks split into 4 categories along (dev/build/management vs session-scaffolding) × (host-user-env vs claude-session-env) axes. A=claude-config dev/build/fmt/test/lint (host, just). B=claude-config management install/uninstall/provision (host, just). C=user's project-local dev tasks (project owner's choice; claude-config takes no stance). D=session-scaffolding env/tools/hooks/steps (claude-session-env, mise as coordinator implementation detail). The taxonomy resolves prior conflation between 'task runner' choices — different categories don't compete.

Sidecar setup/teardown steps shape: each step has name (required), context (host|sandbox, default sandbox), continueOnError (default false, ADO terminology), env (mapping for per-step overrides), dir (optional working dir; relative→project root, absolute→tempfs/bwrap root). EXACTLY ONE of script (inline shell) | run (file path + args, single GHA-style string) | task (mise task name; requires exposeUserMise.tasks=true). Sandbox-context steps are auto-wrapped with 'mise exec --' for env+tool hygiene. Host-context steps run as host user, against canonical tree, BEFORE bind-mount (setup) or AFTER teardown. Wrapper itself is the runner (~20 lines of shell); no DAG/caching since session-init is linear.

Topology lesson from 2026-04-22 auto-session pre-ship bead creation. (1) bd mol/bond is for reusable WORKFLOW TEMPLATES (protos/molecules), NOT for general-purpose epic grouping or taxonomic nesting — use parent-child for hierarchical containment, bond only when composing template workflows. (2) bd enforces a directional constraint: tasks cannot block epics ('epics can only block other epics, not tasks'). Workaround: create a dedicated release-gate task as a child of the epic; route hard 'blocks' edges from external tasks through the release-gate task. Epic-completion semantics then handle the transitive gate. (3) 'discovered-from' edges should trace the actual causal chain — the investigation where a fact was uncovered — not the consumer bead that acts on the fact. (4) For aggregates of one-off work with independent children, a plain epic beats molecule primitives; bd mol distill can promote an ad-hoc epic into a proto LATER if a reusable pattern emerges. (5) Closed-at-creation provenance beads (filing completed research as a task for downstream discovered-from anchors) are a valid and useful pattern — the bead's purpose is audit-trail, not workflow.

autosession-status-transition-mechanics: bd 1.0.2 has no per-transition metadata for status changes other than close (bd close --reason). Non-close transitions use 'bd update --status <new> --append-notes "[autosession/<slug> <ISO-8601>] <from> -> <new>; <rationale>"' in one invocation — notes accumulate in the single notes field, are timestamped by the coordinator-provided prefix, and are filterable via --notes-contains. Respect user's custom statuses (bd config set status.custom review,testing): coordinator default on-finish = review, not closed.

bash-vs-sed-sql-escape: Bash parameter-expansion replacement ${var//\'/\'\'} produces FOUR characters (backslash-quote-backslash-quote), not the two characters ('') that MySQL string-literal escape requires. The backslashes are interpreted literally by bash within the substitution and are passed through into the SQL stream. When escaping single quotes for SQL from bash, use 'sed "s/'/''/g"' instead — sed produces the correct doubled-quote pairs. Discovered while manually provisioning the J121 Dolt user before the J121-ai4 fix landed.

dolt-session-branch-isolation: Each MySQL connection to dolt sql-server has its own branch context. USE `db/branch` at connect time or CALL DOLT_CHECKOUT('branch') switches that session to the target branch. DML and DOLT_COMMIT in that session target the sandbox branch only — verified: inserts on sandbox branch are absent from main. This is the correct mechanism for Tier 2 sandboxing if/when bd adds startup-SQL support. See J121-9kp.2.17.5 findings.

mysql-create-user-sql-escape-pitfall: CREATE USER and ALTER USER need single quotes in IDENTIFIED BY '...' escaped by doubling (' becomes ''), NOT by backslash (\\'). Dolt/MySQL parses '\'' as the literal char ', so a backslash-escaped password is stored with extra characters and authentication silently fails. When the password contains a literal single quote (e.g. pass-generated tokens like '[Ehxs2_g92:Iv>imrL]Sl21'), the quote MUST be doubled before interpolation into the SQL statement. Use Python password.replace("'", "''") or sed s/'/''/g — see J121-ai4.

claude-egress-broker-passphrase-gap: The claude-egress GPG key was provisioned with a real passphrase (4096-bit RSA) stored in the operator's personal pass store at claude-config/egress-broker-gpg-passphrase. The broker CANNOT decrypt credentials until ClaudeConfig hardening Slices B (coordinator-primed gpg-agent via gpg-preset-passphrase) or C (systemd-creds + TPM-sealed credential) land. Until then, the systemd unit's ExecStart will likely exit non-zero (passphrase prompt fails with no TTY/pinentry); the broker is installed and socket-activated but non-functional. Forward-compatible: gpg --passwd can rotate the passphrase without regenerating the key.

Chezmoi feature development happens in the linked worktree at ~/Source/chezmoi-features (created via 'git worktree add' from ~/.local/share/chezmoi). Canonical clone stays on main so chezmoi CLI/daemon stay in sync; feature branches live in the worktree. One branch per worktree is a hard git constraint, so switching features means 'cd ~/Source/chezmoi-features && git switch <branch>' — don't create additional worktrees unless truly parallel development is needed. See project CLAUDE.md 'Session Discipline' section for the full rule.

session-start-orphan-branch-warning-rationale: Filed ClaudeConfig-ob1 (P3) as the engineering backstop to the docs-level no-orphan-branches rule (branching-strategy-2026-06-02 memory). The docs change is the first line of defense; the SessionStart hook would be the second. Three failure modes the hook would catch even if the docs rule were honored: (a) next session skips reading the relevant CLAUDE.md section, (b) orphan branch was created in a prior session whose auto-memory checkpoint isn't surfaced, (c) human author of a required review-bead forgets to file it. Hook design sketch: at bd prime time, for each local branch ≠ main, check whether its tip commit closes a bead (close-trailer or 'Closes X' in message), whether that bead is closed in bd, and whether main contains that commit (git merge-base / git branch --contains). Closed bead + not-on-main + no review-bead-blocking-downstream-work = loud warning. Reason for P3 not P2: docs change may be sufficient on its own — build the hook when the rule fails in practice OR when there are spare cycles. Reason for P3 not P4: this isn't a research spike; it's a real safety net that costs little to implement and addresses a known failure mode caught once already.

Decision 2026-04-20: /show and /next are separate slash commands, not one command with modes. /show = read-only filter+list over bd list/bd ready with ergonomic flag names (--task/--epic/--area/--service/--assignee/--status); /next = scoring/recommendation layered on top of bd ready. Rationale: single responsibility, /show answers 'what exists that matches X?', /next answers 'what should I work on next?' — mixing them forces every /show invocation to load scoring heuristics it doesn't need. Beads: J121-9kp.2.15 (/show), J121-9kp.2.13 (/next). Alternative considered: merge into /next with --filter mode flag — rejected as premature abstraction.

Decision 2026-04-20: /tutor slash command is scoped to the shareable Claude config repo initiative (J121-014), NOT J121-9kp Wave 2. Rationale: in isolation /tutor overlaps with the planned /docs command (J121-9kp.2.4); bundled with the shareable repo it earns its keep as discoverability for users who didn't build the tools themselves. The --provenance flag forces a useful discipline: per-tool source:/decided: frontmatter that skills and commands should probably carry anyway. Expected flags: --short/--long, --provenance, --history, --example[s].

autosession-bead-mutation-schema: Auto-session coordinator uses metadata for cardinality-1 classifications (agent=human|claude|shared set on first claim, autosession_slug=<slug> rewritten each claim — last-toucher semantics, queryable via bd list --metadata-field) and labels only for transient operational state (autosession-deferred, autosession-blocked — added on defer/block, removed on next claim). No accumulating src:autosession-<slug> labels — the full attribution history lives in notes with the required prefix '[autosession/<slug> <ISO-8601>]', filterable via bd list --notes-contains. Rationale: metadata enforces cardinality invariants; labels accumulate and become noise across multi-session re-touches; notes carry transition rationale inline. Aligned 2026-04-21.

bd jira sync --pull incremental timing gap: sync appends AND updated >= <last-sync-timestamp> to JQL, so tickets not updated since the last sync run are silently skipped even if they match the base JQL filter. Root cause of BOCO-18077 missing on initial session sync (2026-04-21). Fix: run 'bd jira sync --pull' again after any period of inactivity to catch stale-but-active tickets; there is no --full-rescan flag. Coverage check: compare 'bd list --label=src:jira | wc -l' against Jira API total. Also: 3 tickets (BOCO-16303, BOCO-17538, BOCO-18128) permanently absent because they were Done before the first full sync ran — not worth importing.

lua-io-popen-exit-code-portability: `file:close()`'s return value varies across Lua versions (5.1 returns just a boolean; 5.2+/LuaJIT-with-Lua-5.2-popen-extensions returns a 3-tuple). For portable subprocess exit-code capture from Lua, append ` ; printf '__rc=%d\\n' "\$?"` to the command and parse the marker from stdout. Used in claude/scripts/hooks/_git.lua per ClaudeConfig-bi0.2.

Decision 2026-04-18: time-tracking billing-tuple storage = Option A (sidecar config .beads/bd-timew.yaml with pattern-based label mappings), NOT Option B (merged |-delimited labels on every issue). Why: per-issue tuple duplication (~95% of issues share the same tuple); NetSuite case strings contain spaces/punctuation that break Beads dimension:value label grammar and are Jira-sync-unsafe; case renames would force bulk relabeling; merged labels defeat the Option B prefix-namespaced label taxonomy. Wrapper: bd start <id> reads issue labels, resolves against sidecar, runs timew start with client:/case:/svc: tags. Convention details in auto-memory feedback-time-tracking.md. J121 sidecar at .beads/bd-timew.yaml (patterns list currently a scaffold - full NetSuite case list still needed).

bd-gc-performance-fix: When bd commands become slow (30s+), the cause is an uncompacted Dolt chunk journal (.beads/embeddeddolt/J121/.dolt/noms/vvvvv... file). Fix: run 'bd gc --skip-decay --force'. Before fix: journal was 27MB, label list-all took ~30s. After: 8.3MB, 0.45s. Weekly cron installed at 'crontab -e' to automate. Root cause: Dolt appends all writes to the journal; reads scan the entire journal until gc compacts it into .darc chunk tables.

beads-batch-commit-config: Beads performance is governed by two settings: (1) dolt.auto-commit — 'on' creates a Dolt commit on every write (causes rapid commit history growth → large oldgen chunk store → slow cold reads); 'batch' accumulates writes in the working set until 'bd dolt commit' is called explicitly. Set via: bd config set dolt.auto-commit batch (persists to config.yaml). (2) Rolling history window: daily cron runs 'bd dolt commit -m daily && bd compact --days 7 --force && bd gc --skip-decay --force' to create one commit/day, prune to 7-day window, and compact chunk store. Gotchas: (a) batch mode leaves writes in working set if process is hard-killed — recovery is 'bd dolt commit'; SIGTERM/SIGHUP flush automatically. (b) 'bd gc' alone (without prior 'bd flatten' or 'bd compact') does not squash commits — it only compacts the chunk journal. History-driven oldgen growth requires 'bd compact --days N' to fix. (c) Performance symptom: slow reads (5-30s) despite small issue count = oldgen .darc has grown from commit history; run 'bd compact --days 7 --force && bd gc --skip-decay --force'. (d) Right after gc/compact, reads are fast due to warm OS page cache; cold reads into oldgen .darc are slower on WSL2 VHD — this is normal.

Per-Bash-tool-call hooks (NOT session-init) live under top-level shellHooks.pre as a list of step-shape entries. Coordinator translates these into mise [hooks.enter] entries in the generated mise config in claude-session's worktree. Requires claude-session's .bashrc to source 'mise activate bash'. Fires on each Bash tool invocation's shell startup + cd-into-worktree. NOT a replacement for setup.steps (mise hooks don't fire when claude exec's directly without an interactive shell). shellHooks.post deferred until concrete use case — mise [hooks.leave] only fires on cd-out, not shell-exit; would need trap injection.

beads-reconcile-design-J121-1b1: Design doc for label-inheritance architecture lives in J121-1b1's description + design fields (bd show J121-1b1). Hybrid C+D+E: (C) bd-timew wrapper forward-propagates inheritable labels to new children, (D) beads-reconcile script walks parent-child edges and materializes missing inheritable dimensions, (E) epics declare the canonical tuple once for org transparency. E alone doesn't work — bd --label is exact-match, doesn't walk graph. See also: billing-labels-on-jira-beads-all-src-jira for the 2026-04-21 manual pass that established the pattern. Resume the design conversation by reading bd show J121-1b1's design field — 7 open questions are captured there.

bv output modifiers and scope flags: --format toon (token-optimized output, lower LLM context usage — works with any --robot-* flag), --recipe actionable (pre-filter: ready-to-work beads only, no blockers), --recipe high-impact (pre-filter: top PageRank scores), --as-of HEAD~N (historical point-in-time analysis, works with any --robot-* flag, e.g. bv --robot-insights --as-of HEAD~30), --label <value> (scope analysis to label's subgraph)
